proposals.title: A Cyclic Degradation Framework for Direct Pore-Pressure Prediction from Seismic Stress-Strain Histories
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction-induced failures during earthquakes remain a critical geotechnical challenge. Current simplified procedures (e.g., CSR-based methods) often lack a direct link to the specific seismic loading history, which governs the complex, path-dependent process of pore-pressure build-up. A more physics-based approach, leveraging computed stress-strain histories, is needed to improve the predictive assessment of liquefaction risk for critical infrastructure like piles and tunnels.
proposals.hypothesis: The cyclic degradation parameters from a 3D plasticity soil model, when calibrated to site-specific conditions, can be used to directly compute cumulative pore-pressure rise from a given seismic stress-strain history, providing a more accurate and mechanistic prediction than empirical cyclic stress ratio methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the researcher's existing 3D cyclic plasticity model (e.g., PM4Sand or a similar framework) by explicitly integrating a pore-pressure generation subroutine. The method involves: 1) Performing nonlinear site response analyses for a suite of recorded ground motions to derive element-level shear stress-strain histories. 2) Using these histories as direct input to the enhanced constitutive model, which tracks the accumulation of plastic shear strain and the associated energy dissipation per cycle. 3) Quantifying pore-pressure rise using a strain-based or energy-based formulation embedded within the model's degradation logic, validated against centrifuge or shake-table test data (e.g., from helical pile experiments).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, mechanics-based computational link between seismic loading and pore-pressure generation, moving beyond empirical correlations. It will deliver a practical modeling framework for assessing liquefaction potential around deep foundations and tunnels, directly informing the seismic degradation analyses central to the researcher's prior work on piles and tunnels.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a pore-pressure prediction mechanism directly into a practical 3D cyclic plasticity model used for seismic degradation, creating a closed-loop analysis from ground motion to liquefaction consequence, rather than treating pore-pressure estimation as a separate, empirical step.

proposals.title: Calibration of a 3D Cyclic Plasticity Model for Seismic Pile-Soil Interaction Using Advanced Laboratory Stress-Strain Histories
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of seismic soil-pile interaction, crucial for the design of helical and driven pile foundations, relies heavily on constitutive models that capture cyclic degradation. Current calibrations often use simplified, monotonic, or low-cycle data, limiting their fidelity for simulating the complex, multi-directional stress paths and degradation observed in real earthquake loading and in shake table experiments.
proposals.hypothesis: That calibrating a three-dimensional cyclic plasticity model (e.g., the PM4Sand or a similar bounding surface model) against advanced laboratory tests replicating derived, multi-directional stress-strain histories from instrumented seismic experiments will significantly improve the numerical prediction of helical pile group response and soil degradation under seismic loading.
proposals.method: The method will first derive target stress-strain histories from instrumentation data of prior shake table tests on helical pile groups. Advanced cyclic direct simple shear (DSS) and hollow cylinder torsional shear (HCA) laboratory tests will be programmed to replicate these multi-directional stress paths. The calibration will employ an optimization framework, coupling the constitutive model with a genetic algorithm or a Bayesian updating procedure to systematically adjust model parameters (e.g., contraction rate parameter, plastic modulus coefficients) to minimize the error between simulated and experimental cyclic backbone curves, degradation rates, and pore pressure generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a rigorously calibrated, high-fidelity soil model specifically tailored for seismic pile-soil interaction analysis. It will provide a validated methodology for translating field or experimental seismic response data into constitutive model parameters, enhancing the reliability of performance-based seismic design for deep foundations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, algorithmic calibration of a 3D cyclic soil model against complex stress-strain histories derived from physical seismic experiments on pile groups, moving beyond conventional calibration against elemental laboratory tests alone and creating a direct feedback loop between system-level response and material model parameters.

proposals.title: Calibrating Laboratory Cyclic Simple Shear Protocols Using Field-Scale Stress-Strain Histories from Seismic Soil-Structure Interaction
proposals.motivation: Current laboratory element tests for cyclic soil degradation, crucial for constitutive model calibration, often rely on simplified, uniform loading protocols that may not represent the complex, multi-directional, and irregular stress paths experienced by soil elements in the field during seismic soil-structure interaction (e.g., around piles or tunnels). This discrepancy can lead to unconservative or overly conservative designs. Validating and constraining these test protocols with real field-scale stress-strain histories is essential for improving the predictive accuracy of numerical models used in performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: Laboratory cyclic simple shear test protocols that are explicitly designed to replicate the amplitude, sequence, and multi-directional characteristics of stress-strain histories extracted from field-scale seismic simulations will produce element-level soil behavior (degradation, pore pressure buildup) that is more representative of the true system response than protocols based on simplified uniform cycles.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled numerical-experimental framework. First, 3D finite element models of field-scale systems (e.g., pile groups or tunnels), using a pre-2026 plasticity model for cyclic degradation, will be subjected to recorded earthquake motions. Stress-strain histories at critical soil elements adjacent to the structure will be extracted. Key metrics (strain amplitude, number of cycles, principal stress rotation) will be statistically analyzed to define a 'representative' loading protocol. This protocol will then be programmed into an automated cyclic simple shear device. Parallel tests using the new protocol and conventional uniform cyclic protocols will be conducted on reconstituted sand specimens. The resulting degradation curves and pore pressure responses will be compared against the target field-scale simulation data for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, data-driven methodology for designing laboratory element tests that are truly representative of in-situ seismic loading conditions. It will yield improved calibration parameters for advanced constitutive models (like the researcher's own 3D plasticity model), directly enhancing the reliability of seismic performance predictions for geotechnical systems. The outcome is a practical bridge between field-scale observations and laboratory characterization.
proposals.novelty: While field data informs model inputs, directly using detailed numerical simulations of soil-structure systems to *prescribe* the loading path for element tests is a novel inversion of the typical calibration process. This moves beyond matching final performance to explicitly matching the elemental stress-strain journey that causes it, a step not systematically undertaken in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: Quantifying Very Low-Strain Site Amplification and Degradation Potential Using Microtremor-Based Scale Analysis
proposals.motivation: Conventional site response analysis, including advanced cyclic plasticity models, is calibrated for engineering strain levels (>0.01%) but poorly constrained at the very low strains (<0.001%) relevant for microzonation and non-destructive testing. The initial, linear site response derived from ambient vibrations is a critical baseline; its deviation under stronger shaking defines the site's nonlinear degradation potential, a key factor in seismic risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A scale analysis framework, adapted from turbulence studies, can be applied to microtremor wavefields to robustly characterize the very low-strain shear wave velocity profile and its spatial variability, providing a foundational state against which cyclic degradation models (e.g., PM4SAND) can be initialized for high-fidelity nonlinear site response prediction.
proposals.method: The research will employ dense arrays of seismometers to record ambient vibrations at well-characterized test sites. The methodology centers on applying the Random Decrement Technique (RDT) to extract deterministic impulse responses from stochastic microtremor data. These responses will be analyzed using a scale-dependent (frequency-wavenumber) decomposition to separate surface waves from body waves and noise. The extracted dispersion curves will be inverted using a Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) approach to obtain probabilistic VS profiles, explicitly quantifying uncertainty. These profiles will serve as direct input to 1D nonlinear ground response analyses using a pre-2026 constitutive model (e.g., the researcher's own plasticity model) to simulate degradation from this validated baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, observationally grounded protocol for establishing the pre-shaking baseline stiffness state of a site, reducing epistemic uncertainty in site response studies. It will directly link non-invasive field measurements to the initialization parameters of advanced constitutive models, creating a more physically justifiable workflow for predicting seismic soil degradation and its impact on infrastructure like piles and tunnels.
proposals.novelty: While microtremor array analysis is established for VS profiling, its formal integration into a scale analysis framework for the explicit purpose of constraining the initial state of cyclic degradation models in seismic engineering represents a significant, non-obvious advancement beyond conventional empirical correlation methods.

proposals.title: Seismic Response of Pile Foundations in Spatially Variable Non-Liquefied Crusts: Numerical and Experimental Investigation
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design for pile foundations in liquefiable soils often assumes a uniform, horizontal non-liquefied crust, which is a significant simplification of real field conditions. Pre-2026 practice and research, including Elgamal's work on cyclic degradation and helical pile groups, recognize that spatial variability in crust thickness, strength, or slope can drastically alter kinematic loading and pile bending moments. This assumption may lead to non-conservative or uneconomical designs, yet a systematic study of its impact is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: Spatial variability in the thickness and strength of a non-liquefied crust during seismic shaking will induce significantly different bending moments and shear forces in pile foundations compared to predictions from uniform-crust models. The presence of a sloping crust interface will further amplify these differential demands.
proposals.method: The study will employ a combined numerical and physical modeling approach. First, a series of 3D finite element models will be developed using an advanced constitutive model (e.g., the PM4Sand model or a multi-yield-surface plasticity model for cyclic degradation) within an open-source framework like OpenSees. The models will incorporate spatially random fields to generate realistic crust variability. Second, a complementary set of centrifuge or shake-table experiments will be conducted on instrumented single piles and pile groups, including helical piles, embedded in layered soil with deliberately varied crust geometry. The experimental data will validate the numerical simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for assessing pile demands under realistic, non-uniform crust conditions, leading to improved design guidelines. It will yield practical charts or correction factors relating crust variability parameters to amplified pile bending moments. The results will directly inform performance-based seismic design for critical infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While soil variability is studied in geostatistics, its explicit coupling with the seismic soil-pile interaction problem for non-uniform crusts is novel. This moves beyond the current state-of-the-art which primarily considers variability in liquefiable layer properties or assumes a uniform crust, directly challenging a key simplification in practice.

proposals.title: Output-Only System Identification for Back-Calculation of Degrading Soil Resistance from Seismic Pile Response
proposals.motivation: Current design and assessment of piles under seismic loading rely heavily on constitutive soil models (e.g., cyclic plasticity) calibrated from laboratory tests, which may not fully capture in-situ, evolving soil-structure interaction during strong shaking. Direct measurement of soil resistance degradation around a pile during an earthquake is practically impossible, creating a critical gap in validating and refining advanced numerical models. This limits the accuracy of performance-based seismic design for deep foundations.
proposals.hypothesis: The evolving dynamic characteristics (e.g., natural frequency, damping) of a pile-soil system, extracted from measured pile response alone, can be inverted to quantify the degradation of lateral soil resistance (p-y curves) during seismic loading. This degradation will correlate with the cyclic degradation parameters of established soil plasticity models.
proposals.method: The research will apply output-only system identification (SID) techniques, such as the Natural Excitation Technique (NExT) combined with the Eigensystem Realization Algorithm (ERA) or Stochastic Subspace Identification (SSI), to acceleration or strain data from instrumented pile shake table experiments (e.g., prior helical pile group tests). These time-varying modal parameters (frequency, damping) will serve as the target for an inverse analysis framework. A finite-element model of the pile-soil system, incorporating a cyclic degradation plasticity model (e.g., the researcher's prior 3D model), will be iteratively updated using a Bayesian inference or optimization algorithm to match the identified dynamic response, thereby back-calculating the evolution of soil stiffness and strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven methodology to quantify in-situ soil resistance degradation directly from structural response, moving beyond purely empirical or laboratory-based degradation rules. It will deliver a validated framework for calibrating and updating advanced soil constitutive models for seismic analysis, enhancing the reliability of performance predictions for piled foundations and tunnels.
proposals.novelty: While SID is mature in structural health monitoring, its application to inversely characterize the *surrounding soil's constitutive behavior*—rather than the structure itself—represents a significant paradigm shift. The fusion of output-only SID with advanced cyclic soil plasticity models for seismic geotechnical inverse problems is a novel, interdisciplinary approach not yet established in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Micromechanical Investigation of Pile-Soil Interaction During Liquefaction Using the Discrete Element Method
proposals.motivation: The seismic performance of pile foundations is critically compromised by soil liquefaction, a phenomenon where saturated granular soils lose strength under cyclic loading. While continuum models capture macro-scale behavior, they rely on phenomenological rules for cyclic degradation and cannot directly simulate the grain-scale mechanisms—such as force chain buckling and pore pressure generation—that govern the loss of soil support around piles. A micromechanical understanding is essential for improving predictive models and designing more resilient foundations.
proposals.hypothesis: The degradation of lateral soil support for piles during liquefaction is governed by the evolution of force chain networks and contact fabric anisotropy at the grain scale, which can be quantitatively linked to the macro-scale cyclic degradation parameters used in continuum constitutive models.
proposals.method: We will employ the Discrete Element Method (DEM) using the open-source LAMMPS or YADE framework to model a saturated soil layer around a rigid pile. The soil will be represented by a polydisperse assembly of spherical particles with contact laws incorporating rolling resistance to mimic realistic sand behavior. A simplified coupled fluid-DEM approach (e.g., the unresolved CFD-DEM method) will simulate pore pressure generation and dissipation. Cyclic shear will be applied to the boundaries to simulate seismic loading, and the micromechanical response—including contact force distributions, fabric tensor evolution, and soil-pile interface stresses—will be analyzed in detail.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, grain-scale explanation for the cyclic degradation of pile lateral capacity observed in shake table experiments. It will yield quantitative relationships between micromechanical state variables (e.g., coordination number, fabric anisotropy) and macro-scale degradation parameters used in practical 3D plasticity models. The findings will inform the development of more physically based constitutive models for seismic design.
proposals.novelty: While DEM has been used to study liquefaction in element tests, its application to explicitly model the complex 3D soil-structure interaction problem around a pile during seismic loading is novel. This bridges the gap between granular physics and geotechnical engineering practice for pile design.

proposals.title: Inverse Reconstruction of Lateral Soil Displacement Time-Histories from Measured Pile Bending Moments Using a Cyclic Degradation-Consistent Framework
proposals.motivation: Direct measurement of soil displacement during seismic events is challenging and sparse, limiting the validation of soil-structure interaction models and the assessment of seismic hazards for deep foundations. Pile foundations, however, are instrumented in many projects, providing bending moment data that encodes the soil's forcing function. A robust inverse method to decode this would transform instrumented piles into valuable subsurface motion sensors.
proposals.hypothesis: The time-history of lateral soil displacement can be accurately reconstructed from measured pile bending moments by solving an inverse problem that incorporates a cyclic degradation soil model, accounting for the hysteretic and stiffness-degrading soil response observed during earthquakes.
proposals.method: The method will formulate the inverse problem using a Timoshenko beam model for the pile coupled with a bounding surface plasticity model (e.g., a 3D extension of the model from Elgamal's prior work) for the surrounding soil. The soil model parameters will be calibrated from site investigation data. The inverse solver will employ an iterative, gradient-based optimization algorithm (e.g., adjoint-state method or Kalman filter variant) to minimize the difference between measured and simulated bending moments, thereby inferring the unknown soil displacement input. Validation will use data from prior shake table experiments on pile groups, including helical piles.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practical methodology for inferring in-situ seismic soil displacement, a critical but difficult-to-measure field quantity. It will advance the interpretation of data from instrumented foundations and provide a new tool for calibrating site-specific ground response models and assessing pile performance under actual seismic loading conditions.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of pile response is well-studied, the explicit inverse problem for soil motion, solved with a sophisticated cyclic degradation soil model consistent with the researcher's previous work, is a novel and non-trivial extension. This moves beyond empirical correlations to a mechanics-based inversion framework.

proposals.title: In-Situ Calibration of Piles as Embedded Sensors for Quantifying Lateral Spreading Magnitude and Distribution
proposals.motivation: Lateral spreading during earthquakes causes catastrophic damage to infrastructure, but direct, distributed in-situ measurement of soil displacement remains a significant geotechnical challenge. Existing instrumentation (e.g., inclinometers) is expensive, sparse, and often damaged during seismic events. Leveraging existing deep foundation elements as sensors could provide a dense, robust, and cost-effective network for characterizing ground deformation.
proposals.hypothesis: The bending moment and strain response of instrumented piles, when interpreted through a calibrated soil-pile interaction model, can be inverted to accurately estimate the magnitude and depth-distribution of lateral soil displacement during liquefaction-induced spreading.
proposals.method: The research will combine physical experiments and numerical back-analysis. First, controlled large-scale centrifuge tests with instrumented pile groups in liquefiable soil will provide benchmark data on pile response to known lateral spreading profiles. Second, a high-fidelity 3D Finite Element model, utilizing a plasticity model with cyclic degradation (e.g., PM4Sand) for soil and nonlinear beam elements for piles, will be developed in OpenSees or a similar platform. An inverse analysis framework will be implemented, coupling the numerical model with an optimization algorithm (e.g., Bayesian updating or a genetic algorithm) to iteratively adjust the input soil displacement field until the simulated pile moments match the measured data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, practical methodology for using instrumented piles as in-situ displacement sensors. It will advance the understanding of soil-pile interaction during lateral spreading and provide a new tool for post-earthquake reconnaissance and performance validation of mitigation techniques. The outcome is a direct link between structural monitoring data and geotechnical hazard quantification.
proposals.novelty: While piles are routinely instrumented for load verification, their systematic use as calibrated sensors to back-calculate the *complete profile* of soil displacement is novel. This approach inverts the traditional soil-structure interaction problem, treating the structural response as the known input to solve for the geotechnical hazard.

proposals.title: Three-Dimensional Nonlinear Seismic Performance and Unseating Risk Assessment of Bridges with Helical Pile Foundations
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design of bridges often decouples foundation, substructure, and superstructure analyses, potentially missing critical system-level interactions that govern collapse mechanisms like deck unseating. The increasing use of helical piles in seismic regions, as evidenced by recent shake table tests on groups, necessitates a holistic 3D framework to evaluate their performance within the complete soil-foundation-bent-deck system under multi-directional shaking.
proposals.hypothesis: The global seismic response and unseating risk of a bridge are governed by the coupled, nonlinear interaction between degrading soil, helical pile groups, and the bridge bent, which cannot be accurately captured by simplified 2D or decoupled analyses.
proposals.method: We will develop a high-fidelity 3D finite element model in OpenSees or a similar platform. The soil domain will be modeled using solid elements with a cyclic plasticity constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand or a user-defined model based on the PI's prior work) to capture degradation. Helical pile groups will be explicitly modeled with beam-column elements and nonlinear p-y, t-z, and q-z springs calibrated from experimental data. The bridge bent and deck will be included with fiber-section elements and gap elements at the supports to directly quantify unseating potential. The model will be validated against the PI's existing shake table data for helical pile groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, integrated 3D modeling framework for assessing the system-level seismic performance of bridges on helical pile foundations. It will deliver new insights into the coupled failure mechanisms leading to deck unseating and produce practical guidelines for considering soil-foundation-structure interaction in the seismic design of such systems.
proposals.novelty: The explicit 3D integration of a sophisticated cyclic soil degradation model with a detailed representation of helical pile groups and bridge superstructure to directly assess unseating risk is a significant advancement beyond typical component-level or decoupled analyses prevalent in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: Validation of Cyclic p-y Models for Piles in Lateral Spreading Using Full-Scale Field Instrumentation and 3D Finite Element Analysis
proposals.motivation: The seismic design of deep foundations in liquefiable soils relies heavily on p-y models derived from laboratory and centrifuge tests. However, a critical gap exists in validating these models, and the advanced 3D constitutive models they inform, against full-scale field performance data from actual lateral spreading events. This lack of validation introduces significant uncertainty in performance-based earthquake engineering for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current state-of-practice p-y models, when implemented within a 3D finite element framework using a plasticity model with cyclic degradation, will underpredict the bending moments and displacements of instrumented piles in full-scale lateral spreading due to inadequate representation of post-liquefaction strength loss and large ground displacement mechanisms.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-tiered numerical validation approach. First, we will calibrate the 3D multi-yield-surface plasticity model (Elgamal et al., pre-2026) against high-quality case histories of instrumented pile groups in lateral spreading (e.g., from the 1995 Kobe or 2010-2011 Canterbury earthquakes). Second, using this calibrated soil model, we will perform 3D finite element analyses in OpenSees to simulate the full soil-pile system response. The computed pile bending moments and displacements will be rigorously compared to field measurements. The performance of conventional p-y models (e.g., API, Boulanger et al.) will be evaluated within the same framework by implementing them as nonlinear Winkler foundations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rare, full-scale benchmark for calibrating advanced constitutive and p-y models for liquefaction-induced lateral spreading. It will deliver quantified recommendations on model bias factors for practice and directly improve the predictive capability of 3D seismic soil-structure interaction analyses for pile foundations.
proposals.novelty: While shake table and centrifuge tests are common for validation, this proposal uniquely leverages hard-to-obtain full-scale field instrumentation data from major earthquakes to test the limits of existing p-y and 3D constitutive models under real-world boundary conditions and soil heterogeneity.

proposals.title: A Cyclic Degradation Framework for p-y Models in Layered Soils Subject to Lateral Spreading
proposals.motivation: Current p-y models for analyzing laterally loaded piles are largely empirical and calibrated for uniform soil profiles. Their application to layered systems with a non-uniform crust overlying liquefiable layers, typical of lateral spreading scenarios, is highly uncertain. This gap hinders the reliable seismic design of deep foundations and tunnels in critical infrastructure projects, as evidenced by damage during past earthquakes.
proposals.hypothesis: A p-y modeling framework that explicitly incorporates the cyclic degradation mechanics of layered soils, including the strength loss in liquefiable layers and the evolving interaction with an overlying crust, will provide a significantly more accurate prediction of pile and tunnel response during lateral spreading.
proposals.method: The research will extend the researcher's existing 3D plasticity model for cyclic degradation to formulate new p-y spring elements. The approach will involve: 1) Conducting 3D finite element simulations of single piles and pile groups in validated soil profiles to generate a benchmark database of soil reaction (p) versus pile deflection (y). 2) Parameterizing the new p-y models using soil layer properties, cyclic shear strain, and excess pore pressure ratios, integrating the degradation logic from the researcher's prior work. 3) Validating the proposed models against centrifuge and shake-table test data, including the researcher's own helical pile group experiments, and implementing them in simplified beam-on-nonlinear-Winkler-foundation analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, mechanics-based p-y framework for layered soil profiles, directly applicable to performance-based earthquake engineering of piles and tunnels. It will provide design engineers with improved tools to assess seismic vulnerability and the efficacy of mitigation strategies like isolation grout for offset tunnels in lateral spreading zones.
proposals.novelty: While p-y models exist, a unified model that captures the coupled degradation of a non-uniform crust and a liquefying layer, grounded in advanced constitutive modeling and systematically calibrated against 3D numerical benchmarks, represents a novel and necessary advancement beyond current code-specified methods.

proposals.title: Coupled Seismic Demand-Capacity Analysis for Retrofitted Geostructures: A Numerical and Experimental Study on Helical Pile Groups and Tunnels
proposals.motivation: Conventional seismic design assumes demand (ground motion) and capacity (structural response) are independent. However, for geostructures like piles and tunnels, retrofit measures (e.g., isolation grout, helical piles) can significantly alter the local soil-structure interaction, thereby modifying the input motion transmitted to the structure. This coupling is not well-captured in current practice, potentially leading to non-conservative or inefficient designs, especially for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic demand on a geostructure is not independent of its retrofit-enhanced capacity; the retrofit system creates a coupled soil-foundation-superstructure system that filters and modifies the input ground motion, leading to a nonlinear interdependence between demand and capacity that must be analyzed integrally.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-pronged approach. First, a 3D finite element model will be developed using OpenSees or a similar platform, incorporating a multi-yield-surface plasticity model (like the PM4Sand model or a similar cyclic degradation model from Elgamal's prior work) to simulate soil nonlinearity and degradation. The model will integrate helical pile groups and tunnels with isolation grout, subjected to recorded earthquake motions. Second, the numerical findings will be validated against existing shake-table experimental data on helical pile group response and tunnel models. The analysis will systematically compare the response spectra at the foundation/structure interface for retrofitted vs. non-retrofitted cases to quantify demand modification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for analyzing coupled seismic demand-capacity in geotechnical retrofit design. It will yield practical guidelines for when coupled analysis is essential and demonstrate the quantitative impact of retrofit measures on both input motion and system response, moving beyond the traditional decoupled assumption.
proposals.novelty: While soil-structure interaction is well-studied, explicitly challenging the independence axiom of demand and capacity for retrofit scenarios—where the retrofit itself re-defines the seismic input—is a significant conceptual shift. Integrating the study of helical piles and isolated tunnels under this unified hypothesis bridges two distinct areas of Elgamal's research.

proposals.title: A BIM-Integrated Framework for Seismic Soil-Structure Interaction Analysis Incorporating Cyclic Degradation and Flexible Foundations
proposals.motivation: Current BIM-to-analysis workflows often assume fixed-base structures, neglecting the critical influence of soil flexibility and cyclic degradation on seismic response. This simplification can lead to inaccurate predictions of structural demands, particularly for systems with deep foundations like piles or adjacent to underground structures like tunnels. A robust, automated integration of soil-structure interaction (SSI) into the digital design workflow is therefore essential for improving the seismic resilience of infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a three-dimensional cyclic plasticity soil model and flexible foundation elements directly into a BIM-derived finite element framework will significantly alter the predicted seismic demands on a structure compared to a fixed-base analysis, and this workflow can be automated to improve design efficiency and accuracy.
proposals.method: The method will develop a plugin for a common BIM platform (e.g., Revit) to extract structural geometry and map it to an OpenSees or similar finite element model. The SSI framework will incorporate Elgamal's pre-2026 3D plasticity model for cyclic soil degradation around embedded foundations. Foundation flexibility will be modeled using beam-on-nonlinear-winkler-foundation (BNWF) approaches for piles, with parameters calibrated from pre-2026 shake-table data on helical pile groups. The workflow will be validated against case studies of structures with isolated foundations and angular offset tunnels.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, open-source tool for automatically generating SSI-aware seismic analysis models from BIM, directly addressing a gap in digital design practice. It will provide a quantitative assessment of how cyclic soil degradation and foundation type (e.g., helical piles) influence seismic performance within a standardized digital workflow.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, automated coupling of a sophisticated, published cyclic soil constitutive model within a BIM-to-analysis workflow, specifically tailored for seismic design of structures with flexible and deep foundations, moving beyond simplified, static soil springs.

proposals.title: Probabilistic Seismic Performance and Loss Assessment of Helical Pile Foundations Using a Suite of Ground Motions and a 3D Cyclic Degradation Model
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design and loss estimation for deep foundations, particularly helical piles, often rely on deterministic analyses using a single or a few ground motions. This fails to account for the significant record-to-record variability inherent in earthquake loading, leading to potentially unconservative or overly conservative designs and unreliable loss predictions. A robust, probabilistic framework is needed to quantify this variability for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: The use of a carefully selected suite of spectrally matched ground motion records, in conjunction with a three-dimensional constitutive model capturing cyclic degradation, will reveal a statistically significant dispersion in the seismic demand and resulting damage/loss metrics for helical pile-supported systems, which cannot be captured by deterministic analysis.
proposals.method: The research will employ advanced 3D finite element modeling in a platform like OpenSees or FLAC3D. The soil will be modeled using Elgamal's established 3D multi-surface plasticity model for cyclic degradation. A validated model of a helical pile group, informed by prior shake-table experiments, will be subjected to a suite of 20-30 ground motion records, scaled to a target spectrum per FEMA P-695 guidelines. For each record, key engineering demand parameters (e.g., pile head displacement, bending moment, soil plastic strain) will be extracted. Statistical analysis (mean, standard deviation, fragility curve development) will be performed on the results to quantify variability in both structural response and derived loss metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated probabilistic methodology for assessing helical pile performance under seismic loading, directly quantifying the impact of ground motion variability. It will produce fragility functions and loss distributions that can inform performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE) for foundations. The results will offer practical guidance for selecting an appropriate number and type of ground motions for reliable design.
proposals.novelty: While probabilistic seismic analysis is common for superstructures, its rigorous application to helical pile foundations, integrated with a sophisticated 3D cyclic soil model, is novel. This study uniquely bridges the gap between advanced constitutive modeling, experimental validation, and probabilistic loss assessment for a modern deep foundation system.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Physics-Informed Neural Network Framework for Predicting the Seismic Response of Helical Pile Groups Incorporating Soil Degradation
proposals.motivation: Current design for seismic pile foundations, including helical piles, relies heavily on complex numerical models (e.g., 3D plasticity models for soil degradation) and costly physical experiments (e.g., shake table tests). These methods are computationally expensive and struggle to provide rapid, accurate predictions for new, unseen ground motions, hindering efficient performance-based seismic design and risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model, trained on a synthesized dataset from validated numerical simulations and existing experimental data, can accurately and efficiently predict key seismic response metrics (e.g., bending moment, settlement) for helical pile groups under new earthquake motions, outperforming conventional purely data-driven approaches by incorporating known physics of cyclic soil degradation.
proposals.method: We will generate a comprehensive training dataset using 3D finite element models, informed by Elgamal's prior cyclic degradation model, to simulate helical pile group response across a wide range of ground motions and soil profiles. This synthetic data will be augmented with existing shake table experimental results. We will develop a hybrid Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) architecture, where the loss function incorporates governing equations for pile-soil interaction and penalty terms for known cyclic degradation trends. This model will be benchmarked against standard deep learning models (e.g., Long Short-Term Memory networks) and simplified analytical solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, computationally efficient surrogate model for rapid seismic assessment of helical pile foundations. It will provide a direct link between advanced constitutive soil modeling and practical engineering prediction tools. The resulting framework will enable probabilistic seismic demand analysis and facilitate the optimization of helical pile group configurations for different site conditions.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to geotechnical problems, the integration of a sophisticated, pre-validated cyclic soil degradation model directly into the neural network's learning objective for pile dynamics is novel. This approach moves beyond black-box prediction to create a hybrid model that respects underlying soil mechanics, specifically for the emerging foundation type of helical pile groups.

proposals.title: A Non-Associated Flow Rule Framework for Cyclic Soil Degradation and Its Impact on Seismic Pile and Tunnel Response
proposals.motivation: Current advanced constitutive models for seismic soil-structure interaction, including the researcher's own 3D plasticity model for cyclic degradation, often rely on an associated flow rule for numerical convenience. This assumption can lead to significant inaccuracies in predicting volumetric strains (dilation/contraction) under cyclic loading, directly affecting the computed seismic response and degradation of critical infrastructure like piles and tunnels. A more physically realistic non-associated flow rule is needed to improve the predictive capability of these models for performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a non-associated flow rule, where the plastic potential function is distinct from the yield function, will provide a more accurate simulation of soil's inelastic volumetric response under cyclic seismic loading, leading to improved predictions of excess pore pressure generation, strength degradation, and the resulting forces and displacements on embedded structures like helical pile groups and tunnels.
proposals.method: The research will extend the researcher's existing 3D multi-yield-surface plasticity model by decoupling the yield function (e.g., a Drucker-Prager or Mohr-Coulomb type surface) from a separate plastic potential function with a different dilation angle. The implementation will be validated against element-level laboratory test data for cyclic simple shear and triaxial conditions. Subsequently, the enhanced model will be deployed in 3D finite element analyses of two key systems from the researcher's recent work: (1) the seismic response of helical pile groups from shake table experiments, and (2) the adverse impact of seismic loading on angular offset tunnels. The simulations will be compared directly with the experimental and prior numerical results obtained using the associated flow rule.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical and validated framework for incorporating non-associated plasticity into advanced seismic soil models. It will deliver quantitative insights into how volumetric strain assumptions influence the predicted degradation of soil strength and stiffness around foundations and tunnels during earthquakes, leading to more reliable design recommendations.
proposals.novelty: While non-associated flow rules are known in soil mechanics, their systematic integration and validation within the context of the researcher's established, complex 3D cyclic degradation model for specific seismic soil-structure interaction problems (helical piles, tunnels) represents a novel and targeted advancement beyond the state-of-the-art pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: A Probabilistic Framework for Context-Driven Fragility Assessment of Coastal Bridges: Integrating Geotechnical Degradation and Structural Response
proposals.motivation: Current seismic fragility assessments for coastal bridges often assume a single governing failure mechanism, such as column flexure, which may not reflect reality in complex soil-structure-fluid interaction scenarios. Pre-2026 research, including Elgamal's work on cyclic soil degradation and helical pile response, highlights the critical influence of liquefaction, scour, and foundation behavior on system performance. A more adaptable framework is needed to probabilistically identify the dominant failure mode under site-specific multi-hazard conditions for effective resilience planning.
proposals.hypothesis: The governing failure mechanism for a coastal bridge under seismic loading is not predetermined but emerges probabilistically from the interaction of site-specific geotechnical degradation (liquefaction/scour), structural detailing, and hazard intensity, with the critical mechanism being the one yielding the highest conditional failure probability for a given scenario.
proposals.method: The framework will employ a modular, multi-mechanism approach. First, separate 3D nonlinear finite element models, incorporating a cyclic plasticity model for soil (e.g., PM4Sand) and advanced pile-soil interaction, will be developed to simulate deck unseating, connection failure, and pile/soil system failure. Second, using a cloud-based or stripe-based analysis, competing fragility curves (log-normal distributions) for each mechanism will be generated via incremental dynamic analysis (IDA). Finally, a Bayesian network will integrate the hazard intensity measure, geotechnical parameters (e.g., CPT-based liquefaction potential), and structural details to compute and compare the conditional probabilities of each mechanism, identifying the governing one.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, system-level fragility assessment tool that moves beyond component-based analysis by explicitly modeling competing failure paths. It will deliver a transparent methodology for retrofit prioritization by identifying the most probable weak link under specific coastal multi-hazard scenarios. The integration of advanced cyclic soil models and probabilistic comparison directly advances performance-based earthquake engineering for geotechnically challenging sites.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in creating another fragility curve, but in developing a probabilistic *selector* of fragility curves. It formally integrates Elgamal's pre-2026 expertise in soil degradation and pile response into a decision framework that treats the governing failure mechanism as a context-dependent random variable, a significant shift from deterministic or assumed hierarchy models.

proposals.title: Constraining Seismic Permeability Evolution via Ensemble Kalman Filter Data Assimilation for Improved Liquefaction Prediction
proposals.motivation: Predicting soil permeability evolution during seismic loading is critical for accurate liquefaction and post-liquefaction flow analysis, yet existing constitutive models are highly uncertain. This uncertainty leads to unreliable predictions of pore pressure dissipation and the timing of strength loss, compromising the seismic performance assessment of foundations and underground structures. A method to constrain these models in real-time with field data is needed to reduce this critical gap in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The uncertain parameters governing permeability evolution in cyclic soil degradation models can be robustly identified and constrained by assimilating real-time pore pressure and deformation measurements, leading to more accurate and stable predictions of liquefaction triggering and consequences than a purely constitutive model-based approach.
proposals.method: We will integrate a cyclic plasticity model with permeability coupling (extending Elgamal's prior 3D framework) into an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) data assimilation system. The state vector will include permeability model parameters (e.g., exponent linking volumetric strain to permeability change). Synthetic and then real data from instrumented centrifuge or field sites (e.g., pore pressure transducer arrays) will be assimilated at discrete time steps during a simulated seismic event. The EnKF will update the ensemble of model states, correcting the permeability evolution trajectory to match observations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, data-constrained framework for predicting in-situ permeability change, directly reducing constitutive model epistemic uncertainty. It will deliver a practical methodology for integrating sensor data into seismic soil-structure interaction simulations, improving the reliability of liquefaction consequence predictions for critical infrastructure like piles and tunnels.
proposals.novelty: While data assimilation is used in geotechnics for parameter identification, its application to dynamically constrain a *state-dependent* property like permeability during the transient, highly non-linear process of seismic loading is novel. This moves beyond static calibration to a continuous, observation-driven correction of the constitutive model itself.

proposals.title: A Parametric Seismic Fragility Framework for Pile-Supported Wharves Based on Morphological Classification
proposals.motivation: Seismic fragility assessment of waterfront structures like wharves is critical for port resilience, yet existing studies are often limited to specific, analyzed geometries. The immense variety of wharf configurations in practice makes comprehensive analysis intractable. This gap leads to significant uncertainty in the risk assessment of unstudied designs, hindering efficient retrofitting and new design optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: Wharf geometries can be systematically classified into distinct morphological families, and within each family, seismic fragility can be accurately modeled as a continuous function of key geometric parameters, enabling the prediction of fragility for novel configurations without full nonlinear analysis.
proposals.method: The research will first develop a morphological taxonomy for pile-supported wharves using clustering algorithms (e.g., k-means, hierarchical) on geometric descriptors like deck aspect ratio, pile slenderness, and pile group layout. For representative archetypes from each family, detailed 3D nonlinear finite element models will be developed using a cyclic plasticity model (e.g., PM4Sand) for soil degradation and calibrated against shake table data. Incremental Dynamic Analysis (IDA) will generate fragility curves. Finally, a parametric meta-model (e.g., Gaussian Process regression or response surfaces) will be trained to predict fragility parameters (median capacity, dispersion) as a function of the key geometric descriptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, generalizable framework for rapid seismic risk assessment of a wide class of wharf structures, moving beyond case-specific studies. It will provide practicing engineers with a practical tool to estimate fragility for new or existing wharf geometries by mapping them into the parametric system, significantly improving port risk management and design efficiency.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of a bioinformatics-inspired morphological classification paradigm to geotechnical earthquake engineering and the explicit formulation of seismic fragility as a parametric surface within geometric families, rather than a discrete set of results.

proposals.title: A Closed-Loop Probabilistic Framework for Site-Specific Seismic Fragility Curves Conditioned on Quantifiable Soil Parameter Variability
proposals.motivation: Current seismic fragility curves for geostructures like piles and tunnels often rely on deterministic or simplified soil profiles, neglecting the significant spatial variability of soil stiffness and strength. This omission leads to unreliable risk assessments, as evidenced by the adverse seismic loading on offset tunnels and the complex response of pile groups. A framework that explicitly quantifies the impact of this variability on structural demand and capacity is urgently needed for critical infrastructure design.
proposals.hypothesis: The distribution of engineering demand parameters (EDPs) and limit-state capacities for soil-structure systems can be robustly predicted by conditioning fragility functions on measurable statistics (e.g., coefficient of variation, scale of fluctuation) of the spatially variable subsurface, leading to more accurate and transparent probabilistic risk assessments.
proposals.method: The framework integrates: 1) Geostatistical simulation (e.g., Sequential Gaussian Simulation) to generate ensembles of 2D/3D random fields for shear modulus and undrained strength, parameterized by site investigation data. 2) Coupled nonlinear finite element analysis using a cyclic plasticity model (e.g., the PM4Sand or a similar model from Elgamal's prior work) for site response and soil-structure interaction (SSI) of representative structures like helical pile groups or tunnels. 3) Probabilistic analysis via Monte Carlo simulation to propagate soil variability into distributions of EDPs (e.g., pile head rotation, tunnel lining strain). 4) Development of fragility functions (log-normal CDFs) where the conditioning variable is a statistic (e.g., spatial COV) of the input soil field.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a practical, end-to-end methodology for generating site-specific fragility curves that transparently account for subsurface uncertainty. It will provide engineers with a direct link between quantifiable soil variability metrics and structural risk, moving beyond qualitative acknowledgments of uncertainty. The resulting framework will be directly applicable to the seismic design and assessment of deep foundations and underground structures.
proposals.novelty: While soil variability is known to influence response, this work novelly makes a specific, measurable statistic of that variability the direct conditioning variable for fragility curves, creating a closed-loop, quantifiable link from site characterization to risk output. It integrates Elgamal's expertise in cyclic soil degradation, SSI, and helical pile response into a unified probabilistic framework.

proposals.title: Seismic Fragility Assessment of Deep Foundations Using Geostatistically Simulated Soil Parameter Fields
proposals.motivation: Current seismic fragility analyses for geotechnical systems often treat soil properties as uniform or as independent random variables, neglecting their inherent spatial correlation. This simplification can lead to inaccurate estimates of structural response and risk, particularly for distributed systems like pile groups or tunnels. A robust method to incorporate realistic, spatially correlated soil variability into nonlinear site response and fragility analysis is critically needed for performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: Fragility curves for deep foundations (e.g., piles, tunnels) derived from seismic analyses that use spatially correlated random fields of key soil parameters (e.g., shear wave velocity, undrained strength) will differ significantly—and be more reliable—than those derived from analyses assuming uniform or independently variable properties.
proposals.method: The research will adapt geostatistical methods from ecology, specifically experimental variogram analysis and sequential Gaussian simulation, to characterize the spatial structure of soil parameters from site investigation data (CPT, boreholes). Multiple realizations of the correlated soil property field will be generated for a given site. Each realization will serve as input for 2D nonlinear finite element ground response analysis using a cyclic degradation constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand). The resulting suite of surface motions will drive structural analyses of representative pile group or tunnel configurations, with responses aggregated to develop probabilistic seismic demand models and fragility curves.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, data-driven framework for integrating geotechnical spatial variability into seismic risk assessment. It will yield new fragility curves for deep foundations that explicitly account for the scale of fluctuation of soil properties. The methodology will advance performance-based design by offering a more realistic representation of geotechnical uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis lies in the direct adaptation of spatial statistics tools from ecology to model geotechnical parameter fields for seismic analysis, moving beyond simple random variables to correlated random fields within a fragility context. This approach explicitly links the geostatistical characterization of site data to the final engineering risk product.

proposals.title: Probabilistic Seismic Fragility Assessment of Helical Pile Foundations Incorporating Spatial Variability of Cyclically Degrading Soil Properties
proposals.motivation: Current seismic fragility curves for deep foundations, such as helical piles, often rely on deterministic or simplified soil profiles, neglecting inherent spatial variability in stiffness and strength. This can lead to inaccurate estimates of system reliability, as evidenced by the sensitivity of pile response to soil conditions in recent shake table experiments. A rigorous, probabilistic framework is needed to quantify how this variability propagates through soil-structure interaction, especially under cyclic degradation, to inform resilient geotechnical design.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial variability of key soil parameters (e.g., shear modulus, undrained shear strength) and their cyclic degradation characteristics will significantly widen the dispersion of seismic fragility curves for helical pile groups, revealing a higher probability of exceeding damage states at moderate intensity measures than predicted by deterministic models.
proposals.method: The study will employ a three-dimensional nonlinear finite element model, incorporating Elgamal's established cyclic plasticity model for soil degradation. A Monte Carlo simulation framework will be implemented, where soil property fields are generated using random field theory (e.g., Karhunen-Loève expansion) with correlation lengths informed by site investigation data. Multiple ground motion records will be applied to each realization. Fragility curves will be developed for specific engineering demand parameters (e.g., pile head rotation) by statistically analyzing the results to relate the probability of exceeding damage states to seismic intensity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated methodology for integrating soil spatial variability into performance-based earthquake engineering for deep foundations. It will yield a new set of fragility functions for helical pile groups that explicitly account for geotechnical uncertainty, offering designers a more robust tool for risk assessment. The findings will clarify which soil variability parameters most critically influence foundation fragility.
proposals.novelty: While random field methods have been applied to static geotechnical problems, their integration with advanced cyclic soil constitutive models for dynamic fragility analysis of innovative foundation systems like helical piles is novel. This moves beyond deterministic degradation studies to quantify the compound effect of spatial heterogeneity and cyclic loading on system reliability.

proposals.title: Vector Intensity Measure Fragility Surfaces for Soil-Structure Systems: Quantifying the Influence of Pulse-Like Motions, Duration, and Spectral Shape on Seismic Performance
proposals.motivation: Current seismic fragility analysis for geotechnical systems often relies on a single intensity measure (IM), typically spectral acceleration, which fails to capture the distinct damage potential of ground motions with different features. This inadequacy is critical for structures sensitive to cumulative damage (e.g., piles in degrading soil) or pulse-like demands (e.g., tunnels), leading to unreliable risk estimates. A robust, multi-feature fragility framework is needed to advance performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic fragility of soil-structure systems (e.g., piles, tunnels) is not sufficiently described by spectral acceleration alone but is significantly conditioned on secondary ground motion features, such as significant duration and pulse indicators. Constructing vector-valued fragility surfaces using these features will yield more accurate and less uncertain estimates of failure probability.
proposals.method: The study will employ a two-stage computational framework. First, a comprehensive suite of nonlinear dynamic analyses will be conducted for representative systems (e.g., pile groups in liquefiable soil, angular offset tunnels) using a validated 3D plasticity model for cyclic degradation. Second, vector probabilistic seismic demand models will be developed, using Sa(T1) as the primary IM and secondary features (e.g., significant duration Ds5-75, pulse period Tp, epsilon) as conditioning variables. Fragility surfaces will be constructed via logistic regression, with sufficiency and efficiency metrics formally evaluated to select optimal IM vectors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a physics-informed fragility methodology that explicitly quantifies how specific ground motion characteristics drive seismic risk for geotechnical systems. It will provide practical vector IM recommendations for performance assessment of tunnels and deep foundations, moving beyond the simplistic near/far-field classification. The resulting fragility surfaces will enable more reliable risk assessments for infrastructure sensitive to different aspects of seismic demand.
proposals.novelty: While vector IMs have been explored for buildings, their application to complex soil-structure interaction problems, explicitly integrating cyclic degradation models and distinct ground motion features relevant to geotechnical failure modes, represents a significant advance. The direct linkage of fragility to measurable, physics-based ground motion parameters beyond Sa(T1) is a non-trivial extension of current practice.

proposals.title: Comparative Seismic Fragility of Helical Pile Groups and Tunnels Under Near-Fault and Far-Field Ground Motions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 seismic design and fragility analysis often treat ground motions generically, yet near-fault motions with distinct velocity pulses and directivity effects impose fundamentally different demands than far-field motions. This oversight can lead to non-conservative or inaccurate risk assessments for critical geotechnical systems like deep foundations and tunnels. A systematic study linking specific ground motion characteristics to system-specific fragility is needed for more reliable performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic fragility curves for helical pile groups and angular offset tunnels will show a significant shift and increased dispersion when subjected to near-fault pulse-like motions compared to far-field motions, due to the exacerbation of soil degradation and dynamic soil-structure interaction effects.
proposals.method: The study will employ advanced 3D nonlinear finite element modeling using a platform like OpenSees, incorporating the researcher's existing cyclic degradation plasticity model for soil. Two validated numerical models will be developed: one for helical pile groups and one for angular offset tunnels. A suite of spectrally matched near-fault (with pulse) and far-field ground motions will be applied. Incremental Dynamic Analysis (IDA) will be performed to derive component and system-level fragility functions (probability of exceeding defined damage states) for each motion type. Statistical comparison of the median (θ) and dispersion (β) parameters will quantify the impact.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide quantitative, system-specific fragility functions that explicitly differentiate between near-fault and far-field seismic hazards. It will deliver practical guidance for incorporating ground motion type into the seismic risk assessment of piled foundations and tunnels. The findings will directly inform the calibration of performance-based design codes and retrofit strategies for infrastructure in seismically active regions.
proposals.novelty: While fragility analysis is established, this study uniquely integrates the researcher's specific expertise in cyclic soil degradation, helical pile response, and tunnel isolation to directly contrast the fragility outcomes for these systems under two seismologically distinct, yet critically important, ground motion types.

proposals.title: Multi-Directional Seismic Fragility Surfaces for Bridges on Liquefiable Ground via Machine Learning Surrogate Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current seismic fragility assessment for bridges often relies on unidirectional fragility curves, which fail to capture the complex, multi-directional nature of real ground motions and their interaction with liquefiable soils. This is a critical gap, as evidenced by the significant damage to bridge foundations during past earthquakes. A robust, computationally efficient framework for multi-directional risk assessment is urgently needed for resilient infrastructure design.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning surrogate models, trained on high-fidelity 3D finite element simulations, can accurately and efficiently predict the coupled seismic response of bridge-ground systems, enabling the generation of multi-dimensional fragility surfaces that explicitly account for the vectorial nature of seismic intensity in liquefiable conditions.
proposals.method: We will develop a 3D finite element model in OpenSees or a similar platform, incorporating a bounding surface plasticity model (e.g., PM4Sand) for cyclic soil degradation and liquefaction, and nonlinear elements for bridge piers and pile foundations. A diverse suite of bi-directional ground motions will be used to perform nonlinear time-history analyses. The resulting dataset of Engineering Demand Parameters (EDPs) and vector-valued Intensity Measures (IMs) will train surrogate models, comparing Gaussian Process Regression for probabilistic output and Deep Neural Networks for capturing high-dimensional nonlinearities. The trained surrogates will then generate fragility surfaces via Monte Carlo simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, computationally efficient framework for generating multi-directional fragility surfaces for bridges in liquefiable sites, directly integrating advanced geotechnical constitutive modeling with machine learning. It will provide a probabilistically rigorous tool for practicing engineers to assess true multi-hazard seismic risk, moving beyond conservative unidirectional approximations.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of 3D cyclic soil degradation models (from Elgamal's prior work) with ML-based surrogate modeling to create explicit fragility *surfaces* for vector-valued IMs is a significant advance over traditional curve-based methods, directly addressing the coupled multi-directional seismic demand in complex soil-structure systems.

proposals.title: A Similitude-Based Framework for Validating Seismic FE Models of Pile-Supported Wharves Using Scaled Centrifuge Tests
proposals.motivation: Validating numerical models for seismic soil-structure interaction, particularly for critical waterfront structures like wharves, remains challenging due to scaling complexities in physical modeling. Current centrifuge testing practices often rely on empirical scaling laws that may not fully preserve all key physical phenomena, leading to potential discrepancies between model and prototype predictions. A rigorous, physics-based similitude framework is needed to ensure centrifuge data provides quantitatively reliable validation for Finite Element (FE) models predicting liquefaction, pile degradation, and system response.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally deriving and enforcing a complete set of dimensionless governing groups (e.g., Cauchy number for inertia/elasticity, Froude number for gravity/inertia, and stress ratios for cyclic degradation) adapted from aerodynamic similitude, scaled centrifuge experiments can produce data that directly and quantitatively validates FE model predictions for seismic wharf performance.
proposals.method: The research will first perform dimensional analysis on the coupled soil-pile-wharf system to identify key Pi-groups governing inertial, gravitational, constitutive, and cyclic degradation responses. A scaling methodology will be developed to map these groups between prototype and centrifuge model, explicitly accounting for the enhanced gravity field. A series of centrifuge tests on a scaled model of a pile-supported wharf will be conducted, instrumented to measure pile bending moments, soil accelerations, and pore pressures. The experimental results will be directly compared against predictions from a 3D FE model, utilizing a cyclic plasticity constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand) for soil, to rigorously challenge the model's simulation of system response and soil degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formalized, transparent protocol for quantitative FE model validation using centrifuge testing, moving beyond qualitative pattern matching. It will deliver a validated, high-fidelity FE modeling framework for assessing the seismic vulnerability of pile-supported wharves, with direct application to performance-based earthquake engineering for ports. The developed similitude principles will be broadly applicable to other geotechnical physical modeling problems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic adaptation of formal wind tunnel similitude principles—a cornerstone of aerospace engineering—to the validation of geotechnical centrifuge-FE model chains, creating a direct, physics-based link between scaled physical tests and numerical simulations for complex soil-structure systems.

proposals.title: Bayesian Field Inversion of Soil Hydro-Mechanical Properties for Seismic Risk Assessment Using Wharf System Response Data
proposals.motivation: The seismic performance of waterfront structures like wharves is critically dependent on the complex, coupled hydro-mechanical response of saturated soils, including pore pressure generation, cyclic degradation, and liquefaction. Pre-2026 practice often uses simplified or deterministic soil models, leading to significant uncertainty in retrofit design and risk assessment for these critical infrastructure systems. A probabilistic framework that directly infers spatially varying soil properties from actual system performance during earthquakes is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatially distributed seismic response and observed damage patterns of an instrumented wharf system, when integrated within a Bayesian inversion framework, can constrain the probabilistic fields of key soil parameters (permeability, compressibility, cyclic strength) and significantly reduce the uncertainty in post-earthquake stability and retrofit analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled finite element model of the soil-pile-wharf system incorporating a cyclic plasticity constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand) for soil degradation. The Bayesian inverse problem will be solved using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, specifically the Transitional MCMC algorithm, to handle the high-dimensional parameter space of property fields. The likelihood function will integrate heterogeneous data: recorded seismic motions as input, in-situ pore pressure and acceleration time histories, and post-event spatial damage maps (e.g., lateral displacement, settlement). The prior distributions will be informed by site investigation data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, data-driven probabilistic framework for characterizing in-situ soil properties under seismic loading, directly linking system-level observations to subsurface uncertainty. It will advance the field of performance-based earthquake engineering for geostructures by providing a rigorous method to quantify soil parameter uncertainty for risk-informed decision-making and retrofit planning of waterfront infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian methods have been applied to geotechnical problems, their integration with full-system, multi-observational field data (seismic, sensor, damage maps) from a complex soil-structure-fluid system to infer spatially correlated hydro-mechanical property fields is a significant advancement beyond common point-estimate or one-parameter inversions.

proposals.title: Minimum-Cost Seismic Retrofit of Pile Foundations via Reliability-Constrained Optimization and Surrogate Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current seismic retrofit design for geotechnical systems, such as pile foundations, often relies on prescriptive codes or iterative trial-and-error, which may not yield cost-effective solutions that explicitly meet performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE) objectives. Decision-makers lack a systematic, automated framework to identify the optimal retrofit configuration that achieves a specific target seismic fragility (e.g., a probability of exceedance for a limit state) at the lowest life-cycle cost, incorporating material, installation, and risk consequences. This gap leads to potentially over-conservative or economically inefficient designs.
proposals.hypothesis: A constrained optimization framework, integrating advanced soil constitutive modeling, reliability analysis, and surrogate models, can efficiently identify the minimum-cost geotechnical retrofit design that satisfies a pre-defined target seismic fragility curve for a soil-foundation-structure system.
proposals.method: The framework will couple a high-fidelity 3D finite element model, incorporating a cyclic plasticity model for soil degradation, with a reliability module to compute fragility curves for candidate designs (e.g., helical pile group configurations, ground improvement schemes). A Gaussian Process or Polynomial Chaos Expansion surrogate model will be trained on simulation data to approximate the fragility surface. This surrogate will be embedded within a gradient-based (e.g., Sequential Quadratic Programming) or evolutionary (e.g., NSGA-II) optimization algorithm, where the objective is to minimize life-cycle cost and the constraint is that the computed fragility does not exceed the target fragility across all intensity measure levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational tool for performance-based geotechnical earthquake engineering, directly linking probabilistic performance targets with economic optimization. It will deliver a practical methodology for engineers to rationally justify retrofit decisions, balancing safety and cost. The integration of sophisticated soil models (like the proposer's cyclic plasticity model) within a rigorous optimization-surrogate framework represents a significant advancement in design automation.
proposals.novelty: While fragility analysis and cost optimization exist separately, their integration into a *constrained* optimization problem, where satisfying a full fragility curve is the constraint, is novel for geotechnical retrofit. Furthermore, the application of this framework specifically to innovative systems like helical pile groups, considering their unique seismic response from recent shake-table studies, moves beyond conventional pile design.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Adaptive Mesh Refinement CEL Framework for High-Fidelity Simulation of Cyclic Soil Degradation and Large Deformations
proposals.motivation: The Coupled Eulerian-Lagrangian (CEL) method is a powerful tool for simulating large soil deformations, such as pile installation and seismic liquefaction flow. However, its fixed Eulerian background grid suffers from numerical diffusion of critical state variables (e.g., porosity, plastic strain), which smears localized shear bands and degrades the accuracy of advanced constitutive models, like your 3D plasticity model for cyclic degradation. This limits predictive capability for problems where evolving material history dictates the global response.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a dynamic, physics-based Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) scheme into the CEL framework will drastically reduce numerical diffusion, enabling accurate tracking of soil state history and localized deformation paths, thereby significantly improving the fidelity of simulations for cyclic degradation and large-strain geomechanics problems.
proposals.method: We will develop an AMR-CEL algorithm within an existing CEL code (e.g., Abaqus/Explicit user subroutines or a custom FE code). Refinement triggers will be based on field gradients of shear strain intensity and cumulative porosity change, directly linked to your cyclic degradation model's internal variables. A hierarchical octree-based mesh will be adapted at regular intervals, refining in zones of high activity and coarsening elsewhere. The advection of all state variables will be managed using a mass-conservative, high-order scheme to preserve material history across mesh changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust numerical tool that maintains computational efficiency while capturing the full evolution of complex soil deformations with unprecedented accuracy. It will directly enhance the predictive modeling of seismic pile-soil interaction (including helical piles) and tunnel response by preserving the spatially varying degradation history. The method will set a new standard for high-fidelity CEL analysis in geomechanics.
proposals.novelty: While AMR is common in fluid dynamics, its integration into geotechnical CEL with triggers based on soil-specific constitutive state variables (not just geometry) is novel. This moves beyond static mesh design or simple strain-based remeshing to a fully adaptive, physics-driven framework for preserving material memory.

proposals.title: Portfolio-Based Seismic Vulnerability Assessment of Geostructures: Quantifying Performance Variability Under a Suite of Realistic Ground Motions
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based earthquake engineering often relies on a limited number of ground motions, potentially overlooking the significant variability in structural response due to different seismic excitation characteristics. This deterministic approach can lead to unconservative designs or missed failure modes, particularly for soil-structure systems like piles and tunnels where cyclic degradation and kinematic loading are critical. A more robust, probabilistic framework is needed to capture the full range of potential seismic demands.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured portfolio of ground motions, representing a realistic distribution of magnitudes, distances, and spectral shapes, will reveal a significantly wider distribution of engineering demand parameters (EDPs) for geotechnical systems than a single or median scenario, and that this variability can be systematically quantified to improve fragility estimates.
proposals.method: The research will employ advanced 3D finite element modeling using a validated cyclic plasticity model (e.g., the PM4Silt or a similar model from Elgamal's prior work) to simulate soil-structure systems such as helical pile groups or offset tunnels. A curated portfolio of 40-60 recorded ground motions will be selected and scaled to match a target conditional spectrum, ensuring representation of diverse durations and spectral shapes. Nonlinear dynamic analyses will be performed for each motion in the portfolio.
