The resulting distributions of key EDPs (e.g., pile bending moment, tunnel lining strain, soil shear strain) will be analyzed statistically to develop probabilistic demand models and system fragilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, portfolio-based methodology for probabilistic seismic vulnerability assessment of geostructures, directly extending financial risk concepts to geotechnical engineering. It will deliver quantitative data on the response variability of pile and tunnel systems, leading to more informed risk assessments and potentially influencing code-based design approaches by highlighting the limitations of single-scenario analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit adaptation of portfolio theory from finance to structure the selection and application of ground motion suites for geotechnical systems, moving beyond simple scaling of a few records to a systematic exploration of performance across a diverse, scenario-consistent excitation set. This integrates probabilistic seismic hazard analysis more directly with nonlinear dynamic response evaluation.

proposals.title: Seismic Performance of Pile Foundations and Tunnels in Soils with Spatially Variable, Non-Horizontal Liquefiable Layers
proposals.motivation: Current geotechnical seismic analysis, including Elgamal's own 3D plasticity model, often assumes idealized horizontal soil layering. Real-world soil deposits, especially alluvial and fluvial environments, exhibit significant spatial variability and non-horizontal, lens-like liquefiable layers. This simplification may lead to inaccurate predictions of deformation patterns, differential settlement, and the seismic response of critical infrastructure like pile groups and tunnels.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial variability and inclination of liquefiable soil layers significantly alter the kinematic demands on deep foundations and the deformation mechanisms around tunnels compared to analyses based on idealized horizontal layering, leading to distinct and potentially more adverse failure patterns.
proposals.method: The research will employ a 3D finite element framework, integrating Elgamal's pre-2026 cyclic degradation plasticity model for soil. Spatially variable soil properties will be generated using random field theory (e.g., Karhunen-Loève expansion) with anisotropic correlation structures to model non-horizontal, discontinuous lenses. A parametric study will be conducted, simulating the seismic response of helical pile groups and angular offset tunnels embedded in these realistic soil profiles under recorded earthquake motions. The numerical simulations will be validated against available centrifuge or shake-table data for homogeneous or layered cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical methodology for incorporating realistic soil spatial variability into performance-based seismic design. It will deliver new insights into the kinematic loading and deformation mechanisms for piles and tunnels in complex geologic settings, moving beyond the limitations of the idealized layered assumption.
proposals.novelty: While random fields are used in geotechnical reliability, their integration with advanced 3D cyclic constitutive models to explicitly study the effects of non-horizontal liquefiable lenses on specific infrastructure (helical piles, tunnels) represents a novel and necessary step towards more realistic seismic performance assessment.

proposals.title: A Scenario-Based Probabilistic Framework for Seismic Performance Assessment of Geostructures Integrating Cyclic Degradation Models
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based earthquake engineering often relies on a single, simplified hazard scenario or a single suite of ground motions, which may not adequately capture the epistemic uncertainty in seismic sources and ground motion characteristics. This can lead to non-conservative or highly variable assessments for critical geostructures like tunnels and pile foundations, especially when soil exhibits significant cyclic degradation. A framework that explicitly incorporates multiple plausible rupture scenarios is needed for robust risk-informed design.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-scenario probabilistic assessment framework, which weights the structural response from scenario-tailored ground motion suites by their likelihood, will produce a more complete and reliable distribution of geostructural performance (e.g., tunnel lining forces, helical pile group displacements) than methods using a single generic motion suite, by explicitly separating aleatory and epistemic uncertainties.
proposals.method: The framework will be implemented for a case-study site with multiple active faults. For each defined seismic source scenario (magnitude, distance, mechanism), we will use established ground motion models (GMMs) and physics-based criteria to select or spectrally match a suite of motions. These suites will drive nonlinear finite element analyses using a pre-2026 constitutive model (e.g., a 3D plasticity model for cyclic degradation) for soil-structure systems representing angular offset tunnels and helical pile groups. Results (engineering demand parameters) from all scenarios will be combined using a logic tree approach, weighting each branch by its seismic hazard contribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, transparent methodology for integrating advanced seismic hazard analysis with detailed geostructural performance simulation. It will deliver specific insights into the vulnerability of tunnels and helical piles under the distinct shaking characteristics of different earthquake scenarios, directly informing performance-based design guidelines. The open-source framework will enable more comprehensive risk assessments.
proposals.novelty: While multi-scenario hazard analysis exists, its direct integration with sophisticated, degradation-capable soil-structure interaction models for specific geostructures (tunnels, helical piles) to produce combined performance distributions is novel. This moves beyond applying a single degradation model to a generic motion set, tailoring the analysis to the physics of each contributing scenario.

proposals.title: Quantifying Seismic Performance Variability of Helical Pile Foundations Using a Comprehensive Ground Motion Suite and a 3D Cyclic Plasticity Model
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design and assessment for deep foundations, such as helical piles, often rely on a limited set of ground motions or a single design-level excitation. This practice fails to capture the significant variability in structural response and soil-foundation interaction induced by the diverse frequency content, duration, and intensity of real earthquake records. A systematic evaluation using a broad suite of motions is essential to move beyond deterministic scenarios and develop more reliable, probabilistic performance estimates for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic response and degradation of helical pile groups, particularly in terms of permanent displacement and bending moment, will exhibit high variability when subjected to a diverse suite of ground motions, and this variability can be robustly characterized by coupling advanced constitutive soil modeling with rigorous statistical analysis of the results.
proposals.method: The research will employ a validated 3D finite element model, incorporating the researcher's existing cyclic plasticity model for soil degradation. A suite of 40-50 recorded ground motions, scaled to a common target spectrum (e.g., for a site in California), will be applied to models of helical pile groups in cohesionless soil. Key response parameters (pile head displacement, bending moment, soil pore pressure) will be extracted for each motion. Statistical methods, including incremental dynamic analysis (IDA) and calculation of dispersion measures (e.g., log-normal standard deviation), will be used to quantify performance variability and fragility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-of-its-kind probabilistic framework for assessing helical pile seismic performance, directly quantifying the uncertainty stemming from ground motion selection. It will yield practical fragility curves and dispersion metrics that can inform performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE) guidelines for helical pile foundations. The study will also demonstrate the integrated application of advanced constitutive modeling and ground motion suite analysis for a complex soil-structure interaction problem.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by the researcher has studied helical pile response and soil degradation separately, this proposal uniquely integrates these themes to explicitly quantify performance variability. The application of a comprehensive ground motion suite and probabilistic analysis to helical pile groups, coupled with a sophisticated 3D cyclic soil model, represents a significant advancement beyond single-scenario or simplified dynamic analyses common in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: A Coupled Eulerian-Lagrangian Framework for Simulating Seismic Soil-Structure Interaction with Large Deformations and Cyclic Degradation
proposals.motivation: Current Lagrangian finite element methods struggle with excessive mesh distortion when modeling large, post-failure soil deformations around deep foundations and tunnels during earthquakes. This limitation hinders accurate prediction of the seismic response and permanent displacement of critical infrastructure. The Coupled Eulerian-Lagrangian (CEL) method, proven in fluid-structure interaction and metal forming, offers a powerful but underexplored alternative for geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a CEL framework with a cyclic plasticity constitutive model will enable robust simulation of large, evolving soil deformations around piles and tunnels under seismic loading, capturing phenomena like gapping, liquefaction flow, and permanent offset more accurately than traditional methods.
proposals.method: The research will implement a 3D CEL analysis in Abaqus/Explicit. The Eulerian domain will represent the soil, modeled with the researcher's existing three-dimensional cyclic plasticity model for soil degradation. Lagrangian parts will represent helical pile groups and angular offset tunnels. Key steps include calibrating the material model against cyclic direct simple shear data, validating the CEL setup against published centrifuge or shake-table test results (e.g., for helical piles), and performing parametric studies on tunnel offset angles and pile group configurations under recorded earthquake motions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, practical numerical framework for analyzing extreme soil-structure interaction problems in seismics. It will provide new insights into the failure mechanisms of offset tunnels and group effects in helical piles under strong shaking, directly informing the design of isolation systems and pile group layouts.
proposals.novelty: While CEL has been used for static geotechnical problems (e.g., penetration), its application to dynamic cyclic loading with a sophisticated soil degradation model is novel. This bridges high-fidelity numerical techniques from other fields with the specific constitutive modeling needs of geotechnical earthquake engineering.

proposals.title: A PIV-Inspired High-Resolution DIC Framework for Validating Cyclic Soil Deformation in Geotechnical Centrifuge Models
proposals.motivation: Validating advanced constitutive models for cyclic soil degradation, such as those developed for seismic loading, requires high-fidelity, full-field deformation data from physical experiments. Current validation in centrifuge testing often relies on sparse point measurements, which are insufficient to capture complex failure mechanisms around tunnels, piles, or slopes. A robust, full-field measurement technique is needed to provide the rigorous experimental benchmark that computational geomechanics lacks, analogous to the role of Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) in validating Computational Fluid Dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the seeding and correlation principles of PIV to soil mechanics, a specialized DIC framework can be developed to reliably capture the full-field, non-linear deformation of soil in dynamic centrifuge tests, providing a direct validation dataset for numerical simulations of cyclic degradation.
proposals.method: We will develop a neutrally buoyant, high-contrast artificial soil speckle pattern using dyed particles matched to the host soil's specific gravity. A high-speed camera system, synchronized with the centrifuge shaker and isolated from vibrations, will capture images. The analysis will adapt a hybrid DIC algorithm, integrating the robust multi-pass window deformation and sub-pixel peak-finding methods from open-source PIV software (e.g., OpenPIV or PIVlab) to handle large, non-linear soil deformations. This framework will be applied to validate 3D finite element simulations of helical pile group response and tunnel isolation under seismic loading.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel experimental methodology for obtaining quantitative, full-field soil deformation data under realistic centrifuge conditions. It will provide the first high-resolution validation datasets for 3D cyclic plasticity models of soil, directly improving the calibration and confidence in numerical predictions for seismic design of foundations and underground structures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct cross-disciplinary analogy, adapting the core seeding and correlation philosophy of fluid dynamics PIV—designed for tracking particles in a deforming medium—to the solid/particulate medium of soil, moving beyond standard surface-based DIC applications in geotechnics.

proposals.title: A Biomechanical Joint Analogy for the Seismic Performance of Bridge Pile Group-Abutment Subsystems: An Integrated Experimental and Computational Study
proposals.motivation: The seismic resilience of bridges is critically dependent on the performance of their pile group-abutment foundations, where complex soil-structure interaction governs load transfer and potential failure. Current design practices often treat the foundation as a monolithic unit, potentially overlooking localized degradation at the critical soil-pile and pile-abutment interfaces. This gap is significant, as cyclic degradation at these 'joints' can precipitate disproportionate damage, similar to failure mechanisms observed in biomechanical implants.
proposals.hypothesis: The soil-pile-abutment subsystem functions analogously to a biomechanical joint, where the primary failure modes are governed by interface mechanics. Isolating and scaling this subsystem for focused study will reveal critical, non-linear load-transfer paths and degradation patterns under seismic loading that are not captured in full-system analyses.
proposals.method: A scaled-down physical model of a pile group-abutment system will be constructed and tested on a shake table, instrumented to measure interface stresses and displacements. This will be paired with a high-fidelity 3D finite element model using a multi-surface plasticity framework (e.g., a modified version of the PM4Sand model) to simulate cyclic soil degradation. The computational model will be calibrated and validated against the experimental data, with a focus on modeling the discrete interfaces using cohesive zone or frictional contact elements to capture the 'implant-bone' analogy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic framework for analyzing bridge foundation resilience by isolating its most critical component. It will deliver validated, high-resolution data on interface-level seismic response and a calibrated computational modeling protocol. These outcomes will advance performance-based seismic design by enabling targeted retrofitting strategies for pile group-abutment joints.
proposals.novelty: The research introduces a paradigm-shifting analogy from biomechanical implant design to geotechnical earthquake engineering, treating the foundation as an assembly of critical joints. This approach deliberately isolates the subsystem, a methodological departure from conventional full-foundation or full-bridge studies prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Seismic Soil-Structure Interaction and Cyclic Degradation in Pile Group-Abutment Systems: An Integrated Experimental and Numerical Study
proposals.motivation: The seismic performance of bridge abutments, supported by pile groups, is critical for overall system resilience. Pre-2026 practice often simplifies this complex soil-pile-abutment subsystem, potentially misrepresenting load paths, group effects, and the impact of cyclic soil degradation. A detailed understanding is essential for retrofitting existing bridges and designing new ones in seismic regions.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic response of a pile group-abutment system is governed by a coupled mechanism where cyclic degradation of the backfill and foundation soils, amplified by group interaction, dictates the system's force-displacement capacity and failure mode.
proposals.method: The study will employ a two-pronged approach. First, scaled physical modeling using a geotechnical centrifuge or large shake table will isolate a representative pile group-abutment subsystem, instrumented to measure bending moments, soil pressures, and displacements. Second, a parallel high-fidelity 3D finite element analysis will be conducted using a constitutive model like the PM4Sand or a user-defined version of Elgamal's practical 3D plasticity model, calibrated against the experimental data. The numerical model will be used to parametrically study the effects of pile type (e.g., helical vs. driven), spacing, and abutment geometry under a suite of earthquake motions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated framework for analyzing the coupled seismic response of pile-abutment systems, quantifying the role of soil degradation. It will deliver practical insights for improving design guidelines, particularly regarding the use of innovative piles like helical piles in abutment foundations, and inform the effectiveness of isolation or ground improvement techniques.
proposals.novelty: While pile groups and abutments have been studied separately, this proposal uniquely isolates their integrated subsystem as the unit of study, explicitly linking cyclic soil degradation models (Elgamal's recent work) with the physical response of helical and conventional pile groups supporting an abutment, a configuration not yet fully explored in shake table experiments.

proposals.title: A Network-Level Seismic Fragility Framework for Transportation Corridors Incorporating Spatially Correlated Geotechnical Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Current seismic risk assessments for transportation networks often treat bridge sites independently, ignoring the inherent spatial correlation of soil properties due to geological deposition. This can underestimate the probability of correlated, multi-bridge failures, leading to non-conservative estimates of network functionality loss. A framework that integrates this geotechnical spatial variability is critical for pre-2026 resilience planning, enabling the identification of system-level weak links for targeted retrofit.
proposals.hypothesis: The explicit modeling of spatially correlated soil property fields (e.g., shear wave velocity, SPT-N) will significantly increase the estimated probability of correlated bridge failures within a transportation corridor during an earthquake, compared to assessments assuming independent or uniformly degraded sites. This correlation is driven by regional geological patterns and liquefaction susceptibility.
proposals.method: The framework will: 1) Generate spatially correlated random fields of key soil parameters (e.g., Vs30, undrained strength) using geostatistical methods (e.g., Karhunen-Loève expansion) informed by site investigation data and geological maps. 2) Employ the developed 3D plasticity model for cyclic degradation to assess site-specific liquefaction triggering and ground response at each bridge location under the correlated fields. 3) Integrate these spatially variable demands into bridge-specific fragility functions (considering pile and abutment response) to perform a Monte Carlo simulation, quantifying the joint probability of multiple bridge failures and network-level serviceability metrics.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a practical, probabilistic methodology for corridor-level seismic risk assessment that directly links geological uncertainty to system performance. It will deliver new insights into how soil spatial correlation governs network fragility, moving beyond component-based analysis. The outcome will be a decision-support tool for prioritizing retrofits and planning emergency response based on geotechnical, rather than solely structural, criticality.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of geostatistical soil modeling with network-scale seismic fragility analysis, explicitly quantifying the impact of cross-correlated geotechnical uncertainty on correlated infrastructure failures—a synthesis not fully addressed in pre-2026 literature which typically focuses on either spatial variability or structural network analysis separately.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Inversion and Probabilistic Simulation Framework for Seismic Liquefaction Deformation Forecasting
proposals.motivation: Current seismic hazard analysis for liquefaction often relies on simplified 1D soil profiles or deterministic 3D models, failing to capture the inherent spatial variability of liquefiable layers and the non-uniqueness of subsurface interpretations from geophysical surveys. This leads to significant uncertainty in predicting ground deformation patterns, which is critical for assessing risks to infrastructure like tunnels and pile foundations during earthquakes. A framework that rigorously quantifies and propagates this subsurface uncertainty into deformation forecasts is urgently needed for improved seismic performance-based design.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Bayesian inversion of surface-based geophysical data (e.g., MASW, ERT) can generate an ensemble of geotechnically consistent 3D realizations of spatially variable soil layers, and that using this ensemble as input for high-fidelity, cyclic-degradation constitutive models will produce probabilistic deformation forecasts with quantifiable uncertainty that are more robust than deterministic approaches.
proposals.method: The method integrates three core components. First, a Bayesian framework using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) or Sequential Monte Carlo methods will invert surface wave dispersion and resistivity data to produce posterior ensembles of 3D shear wave velocity (Vs) and soil unit profiles, constrained by prior geological and geotechnical knowledge. Second, each realization will be imported into a 3D finite element platform (e.g., OpenSees) where the cyclic degradation of liquefiable layers will be modeled using an advanced plasticity model (e.g., PM4Sand or a similar multi-surface model). Third, a sequential data assimilation approach (e.g., via particle filtering) will be developed to update the subsurface model and deformation forecasts as new cone penetration test (CPT) or borehole data becomes available.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, closed-loop probabilistic workflow that directly links geophysical inversion to geotechnical deformation analysis, moving beyond deterministic 'best-estimate' models. It will provide a methodology to quantify the impact of subsurface uncertainty on seismic performance predictions for foundations and underground structures. The framework will also offer a testable, updatable tool for progressive site characterization in engineering practice.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, probabilistic coupling of the geophysical inversion and the high-fidelity deformation simulation within a single, updatable framework, explicitly addressing non-uniqueness. While Bayesian inversion and advanced constitutive models exist separately, their integration to produce probabilistic deformation pattern forecasts from surface data is a significant, non-trivial advancement.

proposals.title: An Immersed Boundary Method for Simulating Large-Deformation Soil-Structure Interaction and Cyclic Degradation
proposals.motivation: Modeling large, cyclic soil deformations around structures like piles and tunnels during seismic events remains a significant computational challenge. Traditional Lagrangian finite element methods suffer from mesh distortion, while fully coupled Eulerian-Lagrangian (CEL) methods are complex to implement. A robust, efficient method is needed to capture the evolving soil-structure interface and soil degradation under extreme loading.
proposals.hypothesis: The Immersed Boundary Method (IBM), adapted from biofluidynamics, can provide a stable and computationally efficient framework for simulating large, cyclic soil deformations and interface degradation by decoupling the soil continuum grid from the structural mesh, thereby avoiding remeshing issues.
proposals.method: We will adapt the classical IBM framework: a fixed Eulerian grid will discretize the soil domain using a pressure-dependent, multi-yield-surface plasticity model (e.g., PM4Sand) to capture cyclic degradation. The structure (e.g., a pile or tunnel lining) will be represented by a Lagrangian mesh of markers. Interaction forces will be computed at the Lagrangian markers and spread to the Eulerian grid nodes using a smoothed Dirac delta function, with soil stresses integrated back to the structure. The implementation will be validated against existing shake-table data for helical pile groups and centrifuge tests for tunnels.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational tool for geotechnical earthquake engineering that efficiently handles extreme soil deformations and evolving interfaces. It will provide new insights into the mechanics of cyclic degradation at soil-structure interfaces and offer a simpler alternative to CEL for problems dominated by boundary interactions, such as pile penetration and anchor pull-out.
proposals.novelty: This represents the first systematic application of the biofluidynamics-inspired Immersed Boundary Method to solid geomechanics problems involving plastic soil deformation and cyclic degradation, creating a new cross-disciplinary analogy for soil-structure interaction simulation.

proposals.title: Validation of Cyclic Soil Degradation and Pile-Tunnel Interaction Mechanisms via Volumetric Deformation Fields in Transparent Soil Centrifuge Models
proposals.motivation: Current validation of advanced constitutive models for cyclic soil degradation, such as the 3D plasticity model developed by Elgamal et al., relies on sparse point measurements or surface-only deformation data from Digital Image Correlation (DIC). This is insufficient for capturing the complex internal failure mechanisms and volumetric strain fields around geostructures like piles and tunnels during seismic loading. A method to obtain full-field, three-dimensional soil deformation under high-g conditions is critically needed to rigorously test numerical predictions of soil-structure interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating matched-index-of-refraction transparent soil with synchronized surface DIC and internal Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) will yield a complete, validated 3D deformation field, revealing previously unobservable internal failure mechanisms and volumetric strain patterns around model piles and tunnels during seismic centrifuge tests.
proposals.method: The research will employ a centrifuge model with a transparent soil analog (e.g., fused quartz or amorphous silica gel saturated with a matched refractive index fluid). The model will include instrumented model helical pile groups and angular offset tunnels. A laser sheet will illuminate internal planes, while high-speed cameras will capture images of seeded tracer particles for internal 2D-PIV analysis. Surface-mounted cameras will perform standard 3D-DIC. The system will be synchronized with a shake table. Data processing will use established openPIV algorithms for internal flow and commercial DIC software (e.g., VIC-3D) for surface deformation, with results fused to construct 3D displacement vector fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, high-resolution experimental dataset of full-volume soil deformation around geostructures under seismic centrifuge conditions. It will directly validate and inform the development of 3D cyclic plasticity and soil-structure interaction models, particularly for problems of seismic degradation, pile group response, and tunnel offset effects. The methodology will establish a new benchmark for physical model validation in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: While transparent soil and PIV have been used in 1-g models, their integrated application with surface DIC in a dynamic centrifuge environment to obtain synchronized internal and external deformation fields is novel. This approach directly targets the critical gap between surface observations and internal mechanisms in physical modeling of seismic soil-structure interaction.

proposals.title: Numerical Assessment of Cyclic Degradation Effects on the Seismic Performance of Helical Pile Groups Supporting Bridge Abutments
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design of deep foundations often relies on simplified models that do not fully capture the progressive degradation of soil stiffness and strength under cyclic loading, as observed in recent shake table experiments. For critical infrastructure like bridges, this can lead to unconservative predictions of permanent displacements and structural damage, particularly for complex soil-structure systems such as pile-supported abutments on sloping ground.
proposals.hypothesis: The implementation of a three-dimensional cyclic plasticity model, capable of simulating soil degradation, into a high-fidelity finite difference (FLAC3D) or finite element (OpenSees) framework will reveal that the accumulated degradation in the backfill and foundation soils significantly amplifies the lateral displacement and bending moments in helical pile groups during strong ground motions, beyond predictions from conventional equivalent-linear or perfect-plasticity analyses.
proposals.method: The research will implement the pre-2026 three-dimensional cyclic degradation plasticity model, previously developed by the PI, into the FLAC3D software using its built-in FISH or C++ interface for seamless integration with structural elements. A detailed 3D model of a bridge abutment on a 2:1 slope, supported by a group of helical piles, will be developed. The model will be subjected to a suite of recorded earthquake motions, and the response will be compared against simulations using conventional Mohr-Coulomb and PM4SAND models to isolate the effect of cyclic degradation. Parametric studies will vary pile configuration, slope angle, and motion intensity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, high-fidelity numerical framework for analyzing full soil-structure interaction problems with cyclic degradation, directly addressing a gap between advanced constitutive modeling and practical geotechnical earthquake engineering. It will yield new insights into the failure mechanisms of helical pile groups in slopes and offer practical guidance for improving seismic design codes for deep foundations in degradable soils.
proposals.novelty: While the cyclic model and helical pile experiments exist separately, their integration into a full 3D soil-foundation-abutment-slope system analysis is novel. This moves beyond element or isolated pile group tests to a complex boundary value problem, explicitly quantifying how localized soil degradation propagates to affect global system performance.

proposals.title: Inverse Calibration of a 3D Cyclic Plasticity Model Using Post-Earthquake Ground Failure and Pile Performance Data
proposals.motivation: Current constitutive models for soil, such as the 3D plasticity model for cyclic degradation, are typically calibrated against laboratory element tests. However, their predictive accuracy for real-world, boundary-value problems like slope failures or pile group response during earthquakes remains uncertain. Leveraging the rich, full-scale performance data from past earthquakes provides a critical opportunity to validate and improve these models for practical seismic design.
proposals.hypothesis: The back-analysis of well-documented field case histories of ground failure and foundation performance can yield a constrained set of model parameters for the 3D cyclic plasticity model that provides more reliable predictions of system-level seismic response than parameters derived from laboratory tests alone.
proposals.method: The research will employ a Bayesian updating framework to solve the inverse problem. Selected case histories (e.g., liquefaction-induced lateral spreading, performance of instrumented helical pile groups) will be modeled using finite elements. The 3D cyclic plasticity model's key degradation parameters will be treated as random variables. An iterative process, using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, will be implemented to find the parameter distributions that maximize the likelihood of matching the observed field deformations and measured pile responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, field-informed calibration procedure for advanced soil constitutive models, directly linking model parameters to observable performance. It will deliver a recommended set of probabilistically defined parameters for use in performance-based earthquake engineering of foundations and tunnels, enhancing the reliability of numerical simulations for design.
proposals.novelty: While inverse analysis is common in geotechnics, its application to rigorously calibrate a sophisticated 3D cyclic degradation model against combined ground failure and deep foundation field data is novel. This moves beyond back-calculating a single factor of safety to updating the fundamental soil model parameters that govern complex seismic soil-structure interaction.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of 2D/3D Wavefield Effects and Heterogeneity on Inferred Soil Stress-Strain Histories for Seismic Design
proposals.motivation: Conventional 1D shear beam site response analysis is a cornerstone of seismic design, but it assumes vertically propagating shear waves in horizontally layered media. This simplification ignores complex wavefield effects (e.g., surface waves, basin focusing) and realistic soil heterogeneity, which can significantly alter the true stress-strain histories experienced by soil elements and embedded structures like piles and tunnels. Validating and improving upon this assumption is critical for accurate application of advanced constitutive models, such as cyclic degradation models, to real-world seismic scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: The stress-strain histories inferred from 1D site response analysis differ systematically and significantly from those generated by 2D/3D wave propagation through heterogeneous soil profiles, leading to non-conservative or inaccurate predictions of soil and structural response.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled numerical-experimental framework. First, we will conduct a parametric study using 2D and 3D finite element models (e.g., OpenSees or FLAC3D) with the researcher's existing 3D plasticity model for cyclic degradation. Models will include idealized and site-specific heterogeneous soil profiles subjected to recorded ground motions. Second, we will design and execute a series of centrifuge or large-scale shake table experiments on instrumented soil models with embedded pile groups, validating the numerical simulations. The stress-strain histories at key locations from 1D, 2D, and 3D analyses will be rigorously compared.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework to assess the error introduced by the 1D assumption for different site conditions and seismic inputs. It will deliver practical guidance, potentially in the form of correction factors or suitability criteria, for when 1D analysis is sufficient versus when higher-dimensional analysis is warranted for reliable design of foundations and underground structures.
proposals.novelty: While the limitations of 1D analysis are known, this study uniquely links multi-dimensional wave propagation effects directly to the inferred soil stress-strain parameters that drive advanced constitutive models, moving beyond simple comparison of surface motions to assess impact on fundamental soil behavior.

proposals.title: Inverse Characterization of Bedrock Excitation and Nonlinear Site Response from Downhole Array Recordings
proposals.motivation: Conventional seismic site response analysis assumes bedrock motion is the known input, a significant limitation when such data is unavailable or poorly constrained. This project addresses a critical inverse problem in geotechnical earthquake engineering: how to deduce the bedrock excitation and the intervening soil column's nonlinear, degrading path effects using only downhole array recordings, which are increasingly available from dense instrumentation networks.
proposals.hypothesis: The nonlinear, hysteretic soil behavior captured in downhole recordings contains sufficient information to inversely characterize both the causative bedrock motion and the frequency-dependent, amplitude-modulated transfer function of the soil profile, even under strong shaking that induces cyclic degradation.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-stage computational framework. First, a Bayesian inverse analysis will be implemented, using the pre-2026 3D plasticity model for cyclic degradation (from Elgamal's work) as the forward model to simulate soil nonlinearity. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling will be used to infer the bedrock motion time series and key model parameters (e.g., shear modulus reduction and damping curves) that best match recorded downhole motions. Second, the methodology will be validated using data from well-instrumented sites (e.g., KiK-net) and applied to interpret data from shake table experiments on pile groups, treating the base input as an unknown to be solved for.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven framework for site-specific seismic hazard characterization where bedrock motion is uncertain. It will advance the practical use of downhole arrays for calibrating advanced constitutive models and improve the accuracy of seismic demand predictions for embedded structures like tunnels and piles by providing better-constrained input motions for design.
proposals.novelty: While system identification and deconvolution are established, this proposal uniquely inverts the full soil constitutive model (including cyclic degradation) to solve for the bedrock excitation, moving beyond linear transfer functions or assumed input motions, which is a fundamental shift in perspective for site response analysis.

proposals.title: System Identification of a Soil Column's Dynamic State-Space Model for Seismic Response and Cyclic Degradation Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current constitutive models for soil, including advanced plasticity models, are often calibrated against element tests and may not fully capture the complex, system-level dynamic response of a soil column during seismic loading. This gap limits the accuracy of predicting the seismic response of embedded structures like tunnels and pile groups. A method to directly identify the in-situ dynamic properties of a soil system from its measured response is critically needed for more reliable performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The dynamic behavior of a soil column under seismic excitation can be accurately characterized as a low-order, time-varying state-space system, where identified modal parameters (frequencies, damping ratios) and system matrices will directly quantify the evolution of soil stiffness and damping degradation during cyclic loading.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled shake table experiments on an instrumented, saturated sand column. Using input base acceleration and recorded acceleration array responses, we will apply Stochastic Subspace Identification (SSI) and Eigensystem Realization Algorithm (ERA) techniques to extract state-space models at discrete time windows. The time-varying identified modal parameters will be correlated with measured shear strains and pore pressures. The degradation trends will be compared to predictions from the researcher's existing 3D plasticity model to establish a cross-validated framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, empirical system identification framework for soil dynamics, moving beyond constitutive model calibration. It will deliver a validated methodology to extract 'black-box' state-space models for soil sites, which can be directly coupled with structural models for piles or tunnels. The results will offer a new benchmark for evaluating and refining advanced constitutive models under realistic boundary conditions.
proposals.novelty: While system ID is standard in structural dynamics, its application to directly characterize a soil column as a dynamic system—particularly to track the evolution of its modal properties during cyclic degradation—is novel. This approach bypasses the need for explicit constitutive law selection at the system level, offering a complementary data-driven perspective.

proposals.title: Validating Centrifuge Modeling of Soil-Structure Interaction Using Field-Scale Seismic Stress-Strain Histories
proposals.motivation: Validating centrifuge models for soil liquefaction and soil-structure interaction (SSI) remains a critical challenge in geotechnical earthquake engineering. While centrifuge testing is a powerful tool, its reliability depends on accurately replicating in-situ stress-strain paths and degradation observed during real earthquakes. This validation gap limits confidence in using centrifuge data to calibrate advanced constitutive models, such as the 3D plasticity model for cyclic degradation developed by the PI, for performance-based design.
proposals.hypothesis: Field-scale seismic stress-strain histories, derived from instrumented sites or validated numerical simulations of past earthquakes, can serve as definitive targets. A centrifuge test series designed to match these targets will successfully validate the modeling of cyclic degradation and pore pressure buildup for specific soil profiles and loading conditions.
proposals.method: The method involves a three-step, coupled numerical-experimental approach. First, select 2-3 well-documented field case histories (e.g., from dense instrument arrays during past earthquakes) to extract target stress-strain-time series at critical depths. Second, design and execute a series of centrifuge model tests on saturated sand deposits, instrumented with accelerometers, pore pressure transducers, and displacement sensors. Third, use the PI's established 3D cyclic plasticity model in a finite element platform to perform a 'virtual centrifuge' simulation of the experiments. The model parameters will be calibrated iteratively until the numerical response matches both the field-derived targets and the physical centrifuge results, creating a closed validation loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, traceable framework for validating centrifuge liquefaction experiments, directly linking field performance, physical modeling, and advanced numerical simulation. It will yield calibrated model parameters and scaling relationships that increase the reliability of centrifuge data for assessing the seismic response of critical infrastructure like piles, tunnels, and foundations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, quantitative use of field-scale seismic stress-strain histories as explicit validation targets for centrifuge experiments, moving beyond qualitative comparison of phenomena. This systematic closure of the field-model-experiment loop for cyclic degradation is a significant advancement over existing centrifuge validation practices.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Earthquake Motions to Target Soil Stress-Strain Histories and Liquefaction States for Advanced Laboratory Testing
proposals.motivation: Current seismic testing protocols for soil-structure interaction (e.g., piles, tunnels) use recorded or scaled motions, which do not guarantee the soil reaches a specific, repeatable state of degradation or liquefaction. This introduces significant variability in experimental results. A method to design input motions that reliably induce a target soil response is needed to standardize tests and directly link soil state to structural performance.
proposals.hypothesis: An inverse analysis framework, coupling a validated cyclic soil constitutive model with an optimization algorithm, can generate synthetic ground motions that, when applied to a target soil profile, will produce a pre-defined stress-strain history or a target liquefaction resistance (e.g., CSR-N curve) in the laboratory.
proposals.method: The method will integrate Dr. Elgamal's 3D cyclic plasticity model (for soil degradation) within an inverse problem framework. A target soil column response (e.g., shear strain time history at a depth, or pore pressure ratio) will be defined. An optimization algorithm (e.g., a genetic algorithm or gradient-based method) will iteratively adjust the frequency content and amplitude of a seed acceleration time series. The forward model, a 1D nonlinear site response analysis (e.g., using OpenSees), will compute the soil response for each candidate motion. The algorithm will minimize the error between the computed and target responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physics-based methodology for designing laboratory and centrifuge earthquake simulations, enabling controlled study of structural (e.g., helical pile groups, tunnels) response to specific, severe soil states. It will advance the field of performance-based earthquake engineering by creating a direct link between a desired soil failure mechanism and the input motion required to achieve it in a test setting.
proposals.novelty: While forward site response analysis is standard, the inverse design of motions to hit a precise soil state—rather than match a spectrum—is a non-trivial, underexplored problem. This approach moves beyond spectrum-compatible motions to *state-compatible* motions, leveraging advanced constitutive modeling in an inverse framework.

proposals.title: Quantifying Post-Liquefaction Stiffness Recovery in Silty Sands Using Downhole Array Data and a Cyclic Degradation Framework
proposals.motivation: While extensive research pre-2026 has focused on the triggering and consequences of soil liquefaction during seismic shaking, the subsequent recovery of soil stiffness and strength—a critical factor for post-earthquake stability, foundation re-leveling, and aftershock performance—remains poorly quantified. This gap limits the ability to predict the evolution of site response and foundation performance in the crucial hours and days following a major earthquake.
proposals.hypothesis: The post-liquefaction re-stiffening process in silty sands can be systematically characterized by a time-dependent recovery function within an existing cyclic degradation plasticity framework, where the rate and extent of recovery are functions of soil density, fines content, and post-shaking drainage conditions.
proposals.method: We will analyze downhole array recordings (e.g., from the Wildlife Liquefaction Array or similar sites) from the mainshock through the subsequent aftershock sequence. Shear wave velocity (Vs) profiles will be derived from cross-correlation of surface and downhole recordings post-shaking, tracking their temporal evolution. This empirical stiffness recovery data will be used to calibrate a novel recovery module integrated into a 3D cyclic plasticity model (e.g., PM4Sand or a similar framework), where the model's degradation parameters are made time- or strain-dependent to capture re-stiffening. The calibrated model will be validated against recorded aftershock responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-of-its-kind, data-driven framework for modeling the time-dependent recovery of soil properties after liquefaction. It will advance performance-based earthquake engineering by enabling more accurate predictions of post-mainshock site response and foundation performance for aftershocks. The results will directly inform the assessment of residual capacity for piles and other deep foundations following seismic events.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the classic liquefaction degradation problem to focus on recovery, leveraging unique downhole array data to calibrate a constitutive model for a phase of soil behavior that is often assumed to be instantaneous or ignored. It explicitly links field-scale seismic observations with advanced constitutive modeling for this post-liquefaction phase.

ideas.title: Network-level seismic fragility of transportation corridors incorporating spatially correlated soil property variability
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to assess the seismic performance of an entire transportation corridor, explicitly modeling the spatial variability and cross-correlation of key soil parameters (e.g., shear wave velocity, undrained shear strength) across multiple bridge sites. By integrating this geotechnical uncertainty into network-scale liquefaction triggering and bridge fragility analyses, the method quantifies the probability of correlated bridge failures and system-level functionality loss. This allows for identifying critical weak links in the network that arise from geological patterns, not just individual structural deficiencies, informing more resilient corridor-level retrofit prioritization and emergency response planning.

ideas.title: Multi-Scenario Probabilistic Seismic Performance Assessment Framework
ideas.description: This framework moves beyond using a single suite of motions from a simplified scenario. It first defines a set of plausible seismic source scenarios and ground motion models that contribute to the hazard at a site. For each scenario, a tailored set of ground motions is developed or selected, capturing the specific frequency content and duration implied by the physics of that rupture. Structural analyses are conducted across all scenario-based suites, and the results are combined using the likelihood of each scenario to produce a robust performance distribution that accounts for both aleatory variability and epistemic uncertainty in the excitation.

ideas.title: Validate FE model via scaled centrifuge tests using wind tunnel similitude principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the well-established principles of similitude and dimensional analysis from aerospace wind tunnel testing to the validation of Finite Element (FE) models for wharves via physical centrifuge tests. In wind tunnels, scaling laws ensure that forces on a model aircraft predict full-scale behavior. Similarly, for a scaled wharf model in a centrifuge, we must formally define the key dimensionless groups (e.g., relating inertial, gravitational, and soil shear forces) that must be preserved. The adapted research idea involves developing a scaling methodology that explicitly maps these similitude requirements, ensuring the centrifuge test validates not just qualitative behavior but provides quantitatively scalable data to challenge and refine the FE model's predictions for seismic performance, liquefaction potential, and structural integrity.

ideas.title: Probabilistic framework for site-specific fragility curves conditional on quantifiable soil parameter variability
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop probabilistic framework that integrates geostatistical models of spatially variable soil stiffness and strength with nonlinear site response and structural analysis. The system explicitly quantifies the impact of this variability on the distributions of engineering demand parameters (EDPs) and limit-state capacities, rather than just noting its influence. The final output is a set of practical, site-specific fragility functions where the conditioning variable is a measurable statistic of the subsurface variability, enabling more reliable and transparent seismic risk assessments for critical infrastructure.

ideas.title: Adapting Spatial Statistics from Ecology to Model Soil Parameter Fields for Seismic Fragility
ideas.description: The original idea is to investigate the influence of soil stiffness and strength variability on fragility curves. A parallel problem exists in landscape ecology, where scientists must model the spatial distribution and variability of habitat properties (e.g., soil moisture, nutrient levels) from limited samples to predict species presence. The analogous solution uses geostatistics, specifically variogram modeling and spatial interpolation (Kriging), to quantify and simulate spatially correlated random fields that respect measured data and observed correlation lengths. Adapting this solution, the research would characterize soil parameter variability not as independent randomness but as a spatially correlated field. By collecting or using existing site investigation data (e.g., CPT logs, boreholes), experimental variograms for parameters like shear wave velocity or undrained shear strength would be developed. These variograms would define the scale of fluctuation (correlation distance). Multiple realizations of the soil property field across a site would then be generated, each serving as input for non-linear seismic ground response analysis. The resulting suite of surface motions would be used to analyze the structural response, ultimately producing fragility curves that explicitly account for the spatial structure and inherent uncertainty of geotechnical variability, leading to more robust risk assessments.

ideas.title: Machine Learning for Multi-Directional Seismic Fragility Surfaces of Bridges in Liquefiable Ground
ideas.description: This research fuses machine learning-based surrogate modeling with advanced 3D seismic analysis to generate multi-dimensional fragility surfaces for bridges subjected to transverse and longitudinal ground motions in liquefiable sites. By training ML models (e.g., Gaussian Processes, Neural Networks) on high-fidelity 3D finite element simulations of coupled bridge-ground systems, the method rapidly predicts engineering demand parameters as functions of vector-valued intensity measures for both horizontal directions. This enables the generation of fragility surfaces that explicitly quantify the probability of exceeding damage states given any combination of transverse and longitudinal seismic intensity, capturing critical multi-directional effects that unidirectional fragility curves miss. The outcome is a computationally efficient, probabilistically rigorous tool for assessing the true multi-hazard seismic risk of infrastructure in complex geotechnical settings.

ideas.title: Scale down to pile group-abutment system: A biomechanical joint analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from biomechanical implant design to study the soil-pile-abutment subsystem. In orthopedics, the bone-implant interface of a joint prosthesis is isolated using scaled models to understand critical failure mechanisms. Analogously, we propose to isolate the soil-pile-abutment 'joint' of a bridge foundation. By creating a scaled-down physical model and complementary computational model, the research will focus specifically on the load-transfer mechanics and potential failure modes at the soil-pile and pile-abutment interfaces, treating this subsystem as a critical, foundational 'implant' within the larger geostructural system.

ideas.title: Probabilistic 3D subsurface constraint and liquefaction deformation modeling via coupled geophysical inversion
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop system to overcome the non-uniqueness of modeling complex soil layers. It first employs a Bayesian framework to invert surface-based geophysical data into an ensemble of probable 3D realizations of spatially variable liquefiable layer geometry. These geotechnically consistent realizations are then used as direct input for high-fidelity deformation simulations, generating probabilistic deformation pattern forecasts. Finally, the framework allows for sequential updating of the subsurface model and hazard output as new site investigation data becomes available, creating a testable and progressively refined prediction tool.

ideas.title: Ground Motion Feature-Specific Fragility Surfaces via Vector Intensity Measure Analysis
ideas.description: This study moves beyond the simplistic near-fault/far-field dichotomy to identify the specific ground motion features (e.g., pulse-like effects, significant duration, spectral shape) that most strongly influence seismic fragility. It employs a comprehensive framework of intensity measure sufficiency and efficiency to construct vector-valued fragility surfaces, explicitly conditioning probability of failure on both primary spectral acceleration and secondary features. The outcome is a robust, physics-informed fragility methodology that quantifies the impact of distinct ground motion characteristics, providing more reliable risk assessments for structures sensitive to different aspects of seismic demand.

ideas.title: Constrained optimization for target fragility: Minimum-cost retrofit design under seismic performance constraints
ideas.description: This research develops a computational framework to find the optimal geotechnical retrofit design (e.g., pile configuration, ground improvement scheme) that achieves a specified target seismic fragility curve at minimum life-cycle cost. The system formulates the problem as a constrained optimization, where satisfying the fragility curve is a reliability-based performance constraint, and the objective function incorporates material, installation, and risk consequences. Advanced surrogate modeling and gradient-based or evolutionary algorithms are employed to efficiently navigate the high-dimensional design space and provide a unique, practical, and economically rational solution for decision-makers.

ideas.title: Apply system identification from structural dynamics

ideas.title: Data-Assimilative, Observation-Constrained Permeability Evolution During Seismic Loading
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a fixed constitutive model for permeability change by embedding it within an ensemble Kalman filter or variational data assimilation system. The model predicts permeability as a function of deformation and pore pressure, but this prediction is continuously corrected against a stream of real-time or near-real-time field measurements from instrumented sites. This directly addresses the constitutive model uncertainty by constraining the most sensitive parameters with direct observations, preventing runaway numerical instability and producing more reliable predictions of liquefaction and its consequences.

ideas.title: Apply Immersed Boundary Method from Biofluidynamics to Soil-Structure Interaction
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting the Immersed Boundary Method (IBM), a technique from biofluidynamics used to model fluid-structure interaction like blood flow in arteries, to the problem of large soil deformations in geomechanics. The analogous IBM solution uses a fixed Eulerian grid for the fluid and a Lagrangian mesh for the structure, with forces coupled via a smoothed delta function to handle large motions without remeshing. The adapted research idea applies this framework to soil-structure interaction, using an Eulerian grid for the continuum soil and Lagrangian markers for the embedded structure or a distinct soil failure surface. This approach aims to efficiently model extreme deformations like pile penetration or anchor pull-out by leveraging IBM's robust coupling mechanism to transfer stresses and displacements, potentially overcoming mesh distortion limits in purely Lagrangian methods while being simpler to implement than full CEL for certain boundary-dominated problems.

ideas.title: Challenge Single Seismic Scenario via a Portfolio of Ground Motions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a single seismic excitation scenario is sufficient for performance assessment by drawing an analogy from Financial Risk Analysis. In finance, portfolio stress-testing uses a suite of varied economic scenarios to quantify investment risk and variability. The adapted solution is to treat ground motions as a 'portfolio' of seismic excitations. A structured suite of motions, representing a range of magnitudes, distances, spectral shapes, and durations, is applied to a structural model. The resulting analysis quantifies the full distribution of engineering demand parameters, moving beyond a deterministic result to a probabilistic assessment of performance variability and system vulnerability under diverse, realistic shaking conditions.

ideas.title: Volumetric Deformation Field Validation via Integrated Transparent Soil and Embedded Sensing in Centrifuge Models
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the surface-only limitation of standard DIC by employing transparent synthetic soils (e.g., fused quartz, amorphous silica gels) and pore fluids with a matched index of refraction. This allows optical access to the interior of the physical model. The system combines surface DIC with laser-sheet illumination and internal PIV tracking of seeded particles or distinct marker layers to measure 3D displacement vectors throughout the soil volume. The synchronized multi-modal data provides a complete, validated deformation field—both internal and external—enabling direct comparison with numerical model predictions of internal failure mechanisms under high-g conditions.

ideas.title: Adapt Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) seeding and analysis for centrifuge DIC validation
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using Digital Image Correlation (DIC) from solid mechanics to validate deformation in centrifuge models. By drawing an analogy from Experimental Fluid Dynamics, we adapt the solution from Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV). In PIV, a fluid is seeded with tracers and a laser sheet illuminates a plane; cross-correlation of successive images yields a full-field velocity map for CFD validation. Adapted to geotechnical centrifuge testing, this involves developing and applying a specialized, high-contrast speckle pattern to the soil model that behaves like neutrally buoyant PIV tracers. Using high-speed cameras synchronized with the centrifuge's motion and adapting DIC algorithms with principles from PIV's robust displacement tracking, we can capture and quantify the complete, often complex, deformation field of the soil. This provides a direct, high-resolution experimental dataset to rigorously validate the predicted deformation fields from numerical simulations in geomechanics, mirroring the role of PIV data in validating computational fluid dynamics models.

ideas.title: A Parametric Framework for Seismic Fragility of Wharf Geometries Using Morphological Classification
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational biology, where the immense diversity of protein structures is managed through morphological classification and parametric modeling. The analogous problem of extending analysis from known to unknown structures is solved by categorizing proteins into fold families and modeling properties as functions of structural parameters. Adapted to wharf fragility analysis, the idea is to first systematically classify wharf configurations into distinct morphological families based on their geometry and pile arrangement. The developed fragility methodology is then generalized into a parametric framework, where seismic fragility surfaces or functions are derived not for a single geometry, but as outputs dependent on key, class-defining geometric parameters (such as aspect ratios, pile slenderness, and connection details). This allows the fragility assessment to be efficiently extended to a wide range of wharf geometries by simply mapping a new design into the classification system and interpolating within the parametric fragility model, rather than requiring a completely new analysis for each configuration.

ideas.title: Context-Driven Fragility: A Probabilistic Framework for Identifying Governing Failure Mechanisms in Coastal Bridges
ideas.description: This research develops a system to determine the critical damage mechanism for a bridge not by a fixed assumption, but through a site- and hazard-specific probabilistic analysis. It integrates geotechnical vulnerability (liquefaction, scour), structural detailing, and hazard intensity to generate competing fragility curves for deck, connection, and pile failure. The governing mechanism for a given scenario is the one with the highest failure probability, creating a more robust and adaptable assessment tool for resilience planning and retrofit prioritization.

ideas.title: Bayesian Field Inversion for Soil Hydro-Mechanical Properties Using Multi-Observational Wharf System Response
ideas.description: This redesigned approach formulates the estimation of in-situ soil permeability and other key parameters (e.g., compressibility, cyclic strength) as a Bayesian inverse problem constrained by multiple, heterogeneous data streams from a wharf system. It integrates spatially distributed damage observations, recorded seismic motions, and any available in-situ sensor data within a coupled numerical model of soil-structure-fluid interaction. The output is not a single value but a probabilistic field of soil properties with quantified uncertainty, directly enabling robust risk assessment and retrofit planning.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement CEL for Evolving Soil Deformation Paths
ideas.description: This redesigned approach enhances the classic CEL method for geomechanics by incorporating a dynamic, physics-based Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) capability for the Eulerian background grid. The system continuously monitors key field variables, such as shear strain intensity and porosity change, to identify zones where deformation is localizing or material history is being poorly resolved. It then automatically refines the Eulerian mesh in these critical regions and coarsens it elsewhere, maintaining computational efficiency while drastically reducing numerical diffusion of soil state variables. This results in a robust, high-fidelity simulation tool capable of accurately modeling the entire evolution of large, complex soil deformations like landslides or pile penetration, from initiation to runout.

ideas.title: Bridge as a Seismic Wave Filter: Adapting Acoustic Metamaterial Design Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts solution principles from the field of acoustics and phononic crystal/metamaterial design to the original seed idea of a bridge altering free-field ground motion. In acoustics, engineered structures are designed to act as filters for sound waves, selectively blocking or passing specific frequencies based on their periodic geometry and resonant properties. The analogous solution involves modeling the structure's unit cell to predict its bandgap and filtering behavior. Adapted to bridge engineering, the idea is to conceptualize the entire bridge system—with its repeating spans, piers, abutments, and foundation elements—as a large-scale seismic metamaterial. The research would develop analytical and numerical models to treat this system as a filter for the incoming seismic wavefield, aiming to systematically predict how it modifies the amplitude and frequency content of ground motion at the abutments compared to the free-field. This approach frames the 'dual problem' not just as passive alteration, but as a predictable filtering phenomenon based on the bridge's design parameters.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Bayesian Joint Inference of Structural Model and Damage State Limits
ideas.description: This approach develops a hierarchical Bayesian framework that probabilistically infers both the parameters of a nonlinear structural model and the associated damage state limits from observational data, such as seismic response records or component test results. The method explicitly models the uncertainty in the pushover relationship itself, preventing bias from an incorrect prior model from being locked in. By jointly learning the system behavior and its performance thresholds, it produces robust, data-informed posterior distributions for damage limits that account for total epistemic uncertainty. This results in more reliable fragility estimates for performance-based engineering and seismic assessment.

ideas.title: Apply Gaussian Process Emulators for Seismic Fragility Surface Generation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of using Gaussian Process (GP) emulators from climate science—where they serve as fast, probabilistic surrogates for expensive Earth System Models—to the problem of generating seismic fragility surfaces. In the analogous field, GPs are trained on a sparse set of high-fidelity model runs to emulate complex, non-linear systems and quantify prediction uncertainty. Adapted to fragility analysis, a GP model would be trained on a strategically sampled set of computationally intensive nonlinear time-history analyses. This surrogate model would learn the mapping from inputs (e.g., spectral acceleration, structural period, strength, detailing parameters) to outputs (e.g., peak drift, damage state). Once trained, the GP emulator can generate the probabilistic demand for any combination of inputs orders of magnitude faster than full simulation, directly facilitating the construction of multi-dimensional fragility surfaces (or 'fragility manifolds') that are essential for performance-based engineering and risk assessment, while rigorously propagating simulation and model-form uncertainty.

ideas.title: Seismic Fuse Analogy for Bridge Damage Sequencing
ideas.description: This idea inverts the pile damage assumption by proposing that deck or connection failure should precede pile failure as the critical damage mechanism. The analogy is drawn from earthquake engineering's 'strong-column weak-beam' and structural fuse concepts, where specific elements are designed to fail first to protect the overall structural integrity. The adapted solution is to engineer coastal bridge superstructure connections as controlled, sacrificial 'fuses' that fail under extreme wave loads, thereby deliberately sacrificing the more easily replaceable deck system to preserve the critical and difficult-to-repair pile foundation, fundamentally shifting the design philosophy for resilience.

ideas.title: Adaptive Domain-Specific Coupling for Soil-Fluid Interaction
ideas.description: This approach introduces an intelligent, criteria-driven simulation framework that dynamically adjusts the degree of solid-fluid coupling during analysis. It begins with an efficient decoupled model for stable regions but embeds real-time monitors for triggers like high pore pressure generation or rapid deformation. Upon detecting these triggers in specific zones, the method locally activates a fully-coupled Biot-type formulation, with careful attention to handshake region compatibility. This provides physically accurate predictions for critical failure mechanisms while maintaining the computational tractability of the original simplified idea for the bulk of the domain.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Stochastic Soil Modeling
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a purely statistical random field by embedding physical and pedogenetic constraints into the spatial stochastic model. The soil property field is generated by a process-based prior that simulates the geological and mechanical formation of soil strata, ensuring realistic spatial correlations and dependencies. A Bayesian updating framework assimilates localized measurement data (e.g., from CPTu, boreholes, or geophysics) to condition the field, providing quantifiable uncertainty. This results in a model that is both stochastic and predictive, capable of simulating realistic soil profiles for unobserved locations and forecasting changes due to natural or anthropogenic processes.

ideas.title: Networked Port Seismic Resilience: Modeling Physical Damage and Functional Cascades
ideas.description: This research develops a simulation model that treats a port as a multi-layer network integrating geospatially distributed physical assets with soft infrastructure dependencies. It subjects the physical layer to spatially correlated ground motions, generating damage states for each asset. These damage states then trigger cascading failures across functional dependency layers (e.g., power loss, IT outage, labor displacement, debris blockage) based on pre-defined logic rules. The output is a probabilistic assessment of post-earthquake port throughput over time, moving beyond static risk to a dynamic measure of operational resilience.

ideas.title: Adapting Tumor Angiogenesis Models for Unsaturated Soil Mechanics
ideas.description: This research seed proposes using advanced constitutive models from unsaturated soil mechanics. Through analogical reasoning, a parallel is identified in the field of Biomedical Engineering, specifically in tumor angiogenesis modeling, where complex multiphase porous media models are used to simulate dynamic, partially saturated biological tissues. The analogous solution involves continuum models that describe interacting solid and fluid phases with saturation-dependent properties. This solution is adapted by transferring the conceptual and mathematical framework of these angiogenesis models to geomechanics. The adapted idea is to reinterpret the phases (soil solid, water, air) within the established angiogenesis modeling structure, leveraging its advanced constitutive laws for phase interactions and its robust numerical solvers to create novel, high-fidelity models for predicting the behavior of unsaturated soils under various hydraulic and mechanical loads.

ideas.title: Adaptive Coarse-Graining for Soil-Fluid Systems Inspired by Molecular Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the monolithic assumption of fully-coupled solid-fluid analysis by proposing a spatially adaptive, multi-resolution approach inspired by hybrid methods in molecular dynamics. The analogous problem in computational chemistry—the intractable cost of simulating all atomistic details in a large solvated system—is solved using coarse-graining and adaptive resolution schemes (e.g., AdResS). Adapted to soil-pore fluid interaction, this leads to a novel computational framework. The method strategically applies fully-coupled poromechanical equations only in localized, critical regions where the coupling is essential (e.g., within shear bands or around penetrating objects). The bulk of the domain is modeled using a simplified, decoupled effective stress method, where the pore pressure field is governed by a coarse-grained diffusion equation parameterized from the coupled model's behavior. A dynamic interface management algorithm, analogous to those in hybrid MD, ensures consistent state variable exchange between zones. This adaptive coarse-graining aims to dramatically reduce computational cost while preserving accuracy where it matters most, enabling larger-scale or longer-term simulations of coupled geotechnical problems.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Active Learning for Adaptive Fragility Surface Generation with Quantified Uncertainty
ideas.description: This system generates seismic fragility surfaces by combining a deep neural network surrogate with a physics-informed learning architecture and an active learning loop. The neural network is trained with a hybrid loss function that penalizes deviations from both simulation data and fundamental physics constraints, such as energy balance. An uncertainty-aware acquisition function then iteratively selects new structural model parameters and intensity measures for high-fidelity simulation, targeting regions of high predictive uncertainty or physical inconsistency. This results in a self-improving model that produces fragility estimates with explicit, trustworthy confidence intervals, is robust to extrapolation, and remains grounded in engineering mechanics.

ideas.title: Model soil stratigraphy as a non-stationary Gaussian process with geophysical covariates
ideas.description: This idea challenges the deterministic soil layering assumption by adapting the geostatistical solution from mineral resource estimation. In that field, ore grade is successfully modeled as a spatially correlated random field using techniques like kriging and Gaussian processes, which quantify spatial autocorrelation via variograms and produce stochastic realizations. Adapted to soil science, we propose modeling soil properties as non-stationary Gaussian processes in 3D space, using geophysical covariates (like EMI or seismic data) as trend components. Sparse borehole data would be used to fit a depth-and-lateral variogram, enabling the generation of multiple equally probable 3D realizations of the soil stratigraphy. This explicitly captures spatial randomness and uncertainty in layer boundaries and property transitions, moving beyond uniform deterministic layers.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Contact Networks to Assess Cascading Bridge Failure Risk
ideas.description: This idea scales up bridge liquefaction risk assessment by drawing an analogy from epidemiology. The analogous field is Epidemiology, where a common solution involves modeling disease spread through contact networks (e.g., SIR/SEIR models on graphs) to assess population-level risk by simulating transmission dynamics based on node susceptibility and edge connectivity to identify critical failure clusters. This solution is adapted by treating each bridge in a transportation corridor as a network node with a quantified liquefaction susceptibility, and traffic flow/rerouting pathways as weighted edges. The research would involve simulating post-earthquake scenarios where the initial 'failure' (liquefaction) of one bridge propagates through the transportation network. This propagation is modeled as cascading failure, driven by the sudden redistribution of traffic loads to neighboring structures and the severing of key logistical pathways, thereby identifying critical bridges whose failure would lead to catastrophic corridor collapse, moving from single-point to systemic vulnerability assessment.
