ideas.title: Adaptive Damage State Calibration via Sequential Bayesian Filtering
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from robotics and autonomous navigation, where Bayesian filtering is used to fuse predictive models with sequential sensor data for state estimation. The analogous problem is a robot's uncertain localization, solved by updating a prior pose estimate (from odometry) with new observations (from LiDAR/cameras) using filters like the Kalman or Particle Filter. Adapted to structural engineering, the pushover-based damage state limits (e.g., drift bounds for different performance levels) are treated as the prior model with associated uncertainty. Observational data from real structures—such as recorded seismic responses, measured residual drifts, or detailed post-earthquake damage assessments—serves as the sequential 'sensor' measurements. A Bayesian updating framework (e.g., a Particle Filter) is then employed to probabilistically update the joint probability distributions defining the damage state thresholds. This process continuously calibrates the fragility models, reducing epistemic uncertainty and evolving generic code-based limits into validated, adaptive, and structure- or class-specific damage state boundaries informed by real-world performance data.

ideas.title: Robust Reliability-Based Design Optimization for Wharves Under Severe Probabilistic Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid Robust-RBDO (R-RBDO) framework for wharf design that explicitly manages both aleatory and epistemic uncertainties in environmental loads and soil-structure interaction. The approach combines multi-fidelity simulation models for computational efficiency with an info-gap decision theory module to quantify a design's robustness to errors or deep uncertainty in the underlying probabilistic models. The optimization process seeks designs that satisfy target reliability metrics while maximizing robustness, ensuring performance under a wide range of plausible real-world conditions, not just a single, potentially flawed, probabilistic scenario. This results in wharf designs that are both probabilistically reliable and demonstrably resilient to gaps in knowledge.

ideas.title: Bayesian Updating of Damage Bounds via Real-Time Reduced-Order Models for Structural Digital Twins
ideas.description: This research fuses aerospace-inspired model order reduction (MOR) with Bayesian updating to create an adaptive digital twin for structural health monitoring. A computationally efficient reduced-order model (ROM), derived from a high-fidelity 3D global analysis, enables real-time simulation of structural response to monitored loading. Observational data from sensors is then used within a Bayesian framework not only to probabilistically update damage state bounds (like inter-story drift limits) but also to update the parameters of the ROM itself, correcting for model form errors and changing structural properties. This creates a synergistic, self-improving system where the model becomes more accurate and the damage assessment more precise as more data is collected, enabling proactive lifecycle management and risk-informed decision-making.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Physics-Informed Learning Framework for Multiphase Porous Media, Transcending Domain-Specific Constitutive Laws
ideas.description: This research develops a generalizable modeling framework for partially saturated porous media that avoids the pitfalls of direct constitutive model transfer. Instead of importing calibrated soil models, it employs the established continuum governing equations from porous media theory as a physics-informed scaffold. The domain-specific constitutive relationships (e.g., water retention curve, relative permeability, effective stress) are represented by neural network surrogates trained on targeted high-resolution data, such as pore-network model outputs or microfluidic experiment results. This approach creates robust, predictive models that are grounded in fundamental physics yet accurately capture the unique microstructure and processes of non-geological materials, from hydrogels to tissue scaffolds.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Global Hazard Analysis via a Climate Model Validation Protocol
ideas.description: This research adapts the model validation protocol from climate science (specifically the CMIP framework) to the problem of validating global analysis techniques for natural hazard assessment. In climate science, models are rigorously tested against historical data using standardized benchmarks and metrics to evaluate performance and uncertainty. The adapted idea is to create a formal, open benchmarking framework where proposed global hazard assessment methods (e.g., for landslide susceptibility, flood risk) are required to be run against a curated, public repository of past hazard case histories. The methods would be evaluated using a defined set of performance metrics, producing a comparative scorecard. This systematic validation would objectively identify the most reliable techniques, quantify their uncertainties, and diagnose systematic biases, thereby strengthening the evidence base for global hazard risk management.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained, Adaptive Reduced-Order Modeling for Robust Global System Prediction
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach creates computationally feasible 3D global models by developing Reduced-Order Models (ROMs) specifically designed for the challenges of Earth system science. It moves beyond direct aerospace transfers by embedding fundamental physical laws (e.g., conservation, symmetries) as hard constraints within a machine learning-based reduction and time-stepping process. The system continuously evaluates its own uncertainty and automatically triggers targeted high-fidelity simulations to retrain and expand its operational envelope, particularly for rare events or nonlinear transitions. This results in a ROM that is both fast for long-term ensemble analysis and provably reliable across a wide range of climatic or geophysical conditions.

ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from climate science, where computationally expensive Earth system models are approximated using statistical emulators (like Gaussian process models or neural networks) trained on ensembles of high-resolution simulations. The analogous problem—making complex, high-dimensional global simulations tractable for extensive parameter exploration—was solved by developing data-driven surrogates that capture the core input-output dynamics. Adapted to the context of 3D global analyses, the research would involve generating a targeted ensemble of high-fidelity 3D simulations, then training a machine learning-based emulator to predict global 3D fields (e.g., physical, chemical, or biological states) from input parameters or forcing conditions. This would enable rapid, computationally feasible uncertainty quantification, sensitivity analysis, and scenario exploration that would be prohibitive with the full model.

ideas.title: A Tiered Framework for Robust, Predictive Validation of Global Hazard Analysis Methods
ideas.description: This research establishes a rigorous, multi-stage validation protocol to move beyond simple retrospective fitting. It first benchmarks proposed global analysis techniques against a standardized, open database of past hazard events. Crucially, it then subjects these methods to a prospective forecasting challenge, where researchers make blind predictions on unfolding or future scenarios to assess true predictive skill. Finally, methods are stress-tested against adversarial synthetic scenarios to identify critical weaknesses and ensure robustness for novel, high-consequence events not seen in historical records.
